segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a bold red screen displaying 'FRONTLINE' as intense, dramatic, and suspenseful background music begins. A male voice states, "Here's Mueller has convinced yet another witness to cooperate." The scene transitions to a nighttime aerial view of Washington, D.C., showcasing the illuminated White House and Washington Monument, with the text 'Originally aired on October 2, 2018' visible. A female voice declares, "The Russia investigation heating up on several fronts," followed by another male voice asserting, "A White House at war." The title 'THE BATTLE' emerges with a red glow over a blurred cityscape, as a male voice questions, "But I say, how do you beat somebody that hasn't done anything wrong?" and another adds, "With itself." A close-up of a newspaper article titled 'The Quiet Resistance Inside the Trump Administration,' featuring a silhouette of the U.S. map, is shown while the female voice describes it as "The stunning op-ed headlined I am a part of the resistance claiming to be a senior official in..." This is followed by a black and white close-up of a concerned Man with Glasses. Multiple male voices discuss the Justice Department, with one mentioning, "Deputy Attorney General Rod Rosenstein talked about possibly invoking the 25th Amendment," and another stating, "Rosenstein has disputed and denied that report." The title 'THE STAKES' appears with a red glow, preceding a dramatically blurred White House. The female voice warns, "If Democrats take control of the House, they are going to be a subpoena generating machine. This White House is going to find itself playing permanent defense." The title 'THE WHOLE STORY' then appears, and the female voice continues, "Trump has been waging a deliberate war on the whole idea that there is such a thing as independent justice." The Woman in Blazer is then seen speaking directly to the camera. A brief shot of Donald Trump at a rally shows him gesturing as his voice is heard saying, "All we hear about is this phony Russia witch hunt. That's all we hear." The White House is shown again, and the segment concludes with Trump speaking from behind a podium in a formal room, as a male voice states, "President Trump is escalating his assault on the..."
segments.visual_original_caption: The video opens with a bold red screen displaying the word 'FRONTLINE' in large white letters, establishing the documentary's branding. It transitions to a nighttime aerial view of Washington, D.C., with the White House and Washington Monument illuminated against the dark sky, setting a serious and political tone. The text 'Originally aired on October 2, 2018' appears in the top right corner throughout. The title 'THE BATTLE' emerges in white with a red glow, followed by a blurred cityscape, then a close-up of a newspaper article titled 'The Quiet Resistance Inside the Trump Administration,' featuring a silhouette of the U.S. map and figures in resistance. A close-up of a man with glasses, appearing concerned, is shown in black and white, followed by the title 'THE STAKES' in bold white letters with a red glow. The White House is shown again, this time with a dramatic, slightly blurred effect, before the title 'THE WHOLE STORY' appears. The segment ends with a woman in a dark blazer and blue shirt speaking, followed by a brief shot of Donald Trump at a rally, then back to the White House, and finally a view of Trump speaking from behind a podium in a formal room with yellow drapes and a large window.

audio_updated_entities.Donald Trump: Appears in two scenes: first at a rally, gesturing with his hand near his face while speaking into a microphone, wearing a dark suit and red tie; later, seated behind a podium in a formal room with yellow drapes, wearing a dark suit and patterned tie, speaking with a serious expression. His voice is heard declaring, "All we hear about is this phony Russia witch hunt. That's all we hear."
audio_updated_entities.Woman in Blazer: A woman with shoulder-length dark hair, wearing a dark blazer over a blue collared shirt, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit setting. Her voice is heard discussing the "Russia investigation heating up on several fronts," the "stunning op-ed headlined I am a part of the resistance claiming to be a senior official in," the potential for Democrats to create a "subpoena generating machine" if they take the House, and how "Trump has been waging a deliberate war on the whole idea that there is such a thing as independent justice."
audio_updated_entities.Newspaper Article: A printed article titled 'The Quiet Resistance Inside the Trump Administration,' featuring a silhouette of the U.S. map with figures in resistance, and text discussing anonymous dissent within the administration, which is described in the audio as a "stunning op-ed headlined I am a part of the resistance claiming to be a senior official in."
audio_updated_entities.White House: Shown multiple times at night, illuminated with spotlights, appearing grand and imposing, sometimes with a blurred or dramatic visual effect to emphasize its symbolic importance. It is described in the audio as "A White House at war" and one that "is going to find itself playing permanent defense."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a woman in a green top speaking directly to the camera with a serious expression in a dimly lit setting, as a male voice immediately states, "investigators who are investigating him." This quickly transitions to Donald Trump, seen from behind at a rally, gesturing toward a cheering crowd while wearing a dark suit and red tie, as the male voice continues, "He's attacking very nature of the Department of Justice. The FBI," followed by another male voice declaring, "It is a disgrace." Dramatic music begins as the scene shifts to a formal meeting room where officials, including a man in a dark suit, are seated around a long table, suggesting a high-level government discussion. A narrator introduces, "From Frontline's award-winning political team," explaining that "Trump viewed the entire intelligence community and the FBI as enemies." The exterior of the J. Edgar Hoover FBI Building is shown, emphasizing its institutional gravity, followed by a man in a suit walking briskly outside, then a close-up of another man in a suit with a concerned expression. The narrator announces, "A two-hour special investigation," and a male voice adds, "And the team Mueller has assembled may be the A-team of prosecutors for an entire generation." A nighttime view of the illuminated Capitol Building and then the White House, both imposing, symbolize the seat of power, as a male voice warns, "You should be afraid, you should be very afraid." Donald Trump is then seen seated behind a podium in a formal room with yellow drapes, wearing a dark suit and patterned tie, speaking with a serious demeanor, while dramatic music intensifies and a narrator states, "Inside the turmoil." A male voice elaborates on "President Trump," describing "the cloud as he calls it," which "hangs over his entire presidency," noting "that he doesn't really understand where it's going, but what's coming next, and if it's coming for him." A black-and-white close-up of a man with a pensive expression, possibly the Man with Glasses, adds introspection, as Donald Trump's voice is heard declaring, "No collusion, no nothing." A helicopter flies over Washington D.C., with the Washington Monument and Capitol visible, reinforcing the national context, while a male voice suggests, "This president may feel empowered to move to either fire Bob Mueller or fire Jeff Sessions or fire Rod Rosenstein or find some way to shut this investigation down." Donald Trump is then seen descending from a helicopter, walking with purpose in a dark suit and red tie, as the narrator announces, "And the showdown with the 45th President." A male voice explains, "The way that the president could be removed if that's the goal is through impeachment and conviction by the Senate or through..." The video concludes with a dramatic, blurred shot of the White House at night, followed by a close-up of Trump’s face looking upward with a solemn expression, and ends with American flags waving in the wind, symbolizing patriotism and national tension. Throughout, the on-screen text 'Originally aired on October 2, 2018' and the PBS FRONTLINE logo remain visible.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a man with glasses and a beard, wearing a dark suit and red tie, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit setting, stating that "the American people... are going to decide Trump's fate," setting a serious or reflective tone. This transitions to a dramatic black-and-white close-up of Donald Trump’s face, focusing on his eyes and expression, conveying intensity and contemplation, as urgent soundbites declare "fireworks on Capitol Hill," a "firestorm hovering over the White House," and that "the stakes could not be higher," accompanied by dramatic music. A deep male voiceover then announces "Trump's showdown." The scene shifts to a sweeping aerial view of New York City at dusk, with the Empire State Building illuminated against a fiery sky, and a timestamp indicating 'January 6, 2017' appears, while news reports discuss "the news that Michael Cohen made yesterday" and an "extraordinary moment in American history." The camera glides through the cityscape, capturing towering skyscrapers and bustling streets, as a female news anchor reports on "today's showdown at Trump Tower between the President-elect and top intelligence," followed by a male anchor stating "President-elect Trump is about to get all of the details from US intelligence." This leads to a silhouette of a man in a suit standing before three bright vertical windows, evoking a sense of isolation or decision-making, as another male anchor explains that "intelligence officials are expected to meet face-to-face with President-elect Trump at Trump Tower two weeks before the inauguration for what could be a day of fireworks," and that "the battle lines between the new President and Washington's establishment were about to be drawn." This is followed by a black-and-white profile shot of Donald Trump, emphasizing his features in stark contrast. The video then introduces John Brennan, identified as 'Fmr. CIA Director,' seated in a formal interview setting, wearing a dark suit and patterned tie, speaking with a serious demeanor about his concern that "Mr. Trump was ill-prepared for the job," lacking a "good grasp of international affairs, the legislative process, US law, intelligence capabilities," and mentioning a "highly classified report." The final scene shows a chaotic street scene with emergency vehicles, including a Highway Patrol truck with flashing red lights, surrounded by barriers and activity, suggesting a high-security or crisis situation, as the dramatic music continues.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a chaotic urban emergency scene, featuring a black SUV with flashing red and blue lights, surrounded by barriers and other emergency vehicles, including a Highway Patrol truck and an FDNY ambulance, all set to a tense, dramatic underscore. Armed police officers in tactical gear, including helmets and rifles, are seen exiting a building labeled '725' and moving with urgency, indicating a law enforcement operation. Michael Isikoff, Co-Author of 'Russian Roulette,' appears in a formal interview setting, wearing a suit and red tie, speaking with a serious expression about "Russia's hacking of US political institutions." A narrator then states that "four of the most powerful men in the United States government arrived" "on the street, far below." Isikoff continues, describing "a great deal of apprehension" as "the intelligence chiefs" went in, "knowing that their audience is skeptical of what they're about to say." The scene shifts to a partially obscured man in a car, followed by a black-and-white montage of men in suits, including a bald man with glasses and another with a stern expression, walking through a corridor, evoking official gravity. The narrator identifies these as "senior leaders of a group known as the IC, the intelligence community," specifically naming James Clapper as "the Director of National Intelligence," Admiral Mike Rogers as "in charge of the NSA," Jim Comey as "the director of the FBI," and John Brennan as having "ran the CIA." A close-up of a glowing orange light on a wooden surface is shown, followed by a digital display of the number '4' in blue, possibly indicating a countdown or alert. The narrator explains that "they had come to tell Donald Trump that his election may have been compromised by Russian interference." The segment concludes with James Clapper, Fmr. Dir. of Natl. Intelligence, seen in a formal interview setting, wearing a dark suit and patterned tie with a serious demeanor, as Michael Isikoff's voice returns, describing the situation as "the most aggressive, most direct, and most assertive."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of John Brennan, identified as 'Fmr. CIA Director,' speaking seriously in a dimly lit interview setting, wearing a dark suit and a patterned tie. He states that a Russian campaign was the most significant "that the Russians ever mounted in the history of our elections... to interfere and and to somehow influence the outcome." A subtle, serious background music plays throughout the segment. The scene transitions to a dramatic black-and-white profile shot of Donald Trump, illuminated from the side, as a narrator's voice explains, "Behind closed doors, the IC briefing began." James Clapper, 'Fmr. Dir. of Natl. Intelligence,' is then seen in a formal interview, wearing a dark suit and a purple polka-dot tie, gesturing with his hands as he speaks, detailing that the briefing "was several hours long. There was no equivocation in our language, and we were very uh direct and uh very uh very clear in terms of what it is that uh we knew and assessed." The video cuts back to John Brennan, continuing his testimony with a focused expression, asserting, "There was no pushback... because... the evidence that we laid out at the highly highly classified level was pretty pretty compelling and been very hard to to have pushback." Following this, another black-and-white close-up of Donald Trump appears, this time with his hand resting on his forehead, conveying deep thought or concern, as the narrator explains, "Trump didn't argue, but he later said he saw the talk of Russian interference as an assault on the legitimacy of his victory." The final shot introduces Sarah Ellison, a reporter from The Washington Post, identified by on-screen text, speaking directly to the camera in a warm-lit setting, wearing a purple blouse, her expression engaged and articulate, as she comments, "It's a challenge not just his legitimacy as president, but as his overall power, his overall sort of sense of..." The overall tone is serious and investigative, with alternating expert commentary and symbolic imagery of Trump.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a series of black-and-white close-ups, first showing a bald Man with Glasses in a suit with a serious expression, followed by Donald Trump in a dramatically lit side-profile, then with his hand on his forehead conveying distress or contemplation. A narrator explains that "The IC Chiefs had one more piece of news for the President-elect," and after Brennan, Clapper, and Rogers left, "James Comey stayed behind to deliver it." The scene then shifts to a color interview with Matt Apuzzo from The New York Times, who, speaking directly to the camera in a warmly lit room, recounts how "Comey pulls the President aside and he tells him, 'Hey, listen, I need you to know that there's this what we now call the dossier.'" A montage of redacted documents appears, with text highlighting claims about Russian efforts, as the narrator describes "The dossier, a set of memos prepared by a former British spy, partially paid for by the Democrats," calling it "political dynamite." The narrator continues, reading from the document, that the "Russian regime has been cultivating, supporting, and assisting Trump for at least five years." Jane Mayer from The New Yorker, seen in a dimly lit setting, adds that the dossier is "full of things that may be able to allow the Russians to blackmail him," and "has information about him involved in perverted sexual acts." The documents are shown again, with key phrases like 'sexual perversion' and 'compromising material' underlined in red, as the narrator emphasizes the intent "to exploit Trump's personal obsessions and sexual perversion in order to obtain suitable compromat, compromat."
segments.visual_original_caption: The video opens with a series of black-and-white close-ups: first, a bald man with glasses (Man with Glasses) in a suit, shown in profile with a serious expression, suggesting a formal or governmental setting. This transitions to a close-up of Donald Trump in a side-profile, illuminated dramatically, followed by a shot of him with his hand on his forehead, conveying distress or contemplation. The scene then shifts to a color interview with Matt Apuzzo from The New York Times, who speaks directly to the camera in a warmly lit room, wearing a dark blazer over a patterned shirt. The video cuts to a montage of redacted documents, with text highlighting claims about Russian efforts to cultivate Donald Trump over five years and exploit his personal obsessions for compromising material. A close-up of one document emphasizes the phrase 'sexual perversion' and 'compromising material.' The segment then features Jane Mayer from The New Yorker, speaking in a dimly lit setting, wearing a dark blazer over a blue collared shirt, her expression serious as she discusses the content. The documents are shown again, with key phrases underlined in red, reinforcing the gravity of the allegations.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a close-up of a redacted newspaper article, its key phrases underlined in red, detailing allegations of Russian efforts to exploit Donald Trump's personal obsessions and sexual perversion for 'compromising material.' A male narrator with a serious, dramatic tone reads aloud from the article, describing 'salacious and unverified allegations' involving Trump and Russian prostitutes in a Moscow hotel suite. The scene transitions to Carrie Johnson from NPR, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit setting, expressing concern with a clear, slightly worried voice, 'Whoa, not great, not a great stretch of this relationship. And Comey worries about that.' A dramatic black-and-white close-up of Robert Mueller's intense face follows as the narrator's voice returns, stating Comey had been warned not to appear to threaten Trump. Next, Jeh Johnson, former Secretary of Homeland Security, appears in a formal interview setting, recounting a conversation in a deliberate, formal tone: 'I called Jim, I got him on the phone. I said, 'Jim, have you ever met Donald Trump before?' And a little to my surprise, he said, 'No, I have not.' I said, 'Jim, you're in a very awkward spot here.'' He continues, emphasizing a 'fine distinction' between simply informing someone and a more forceful warning, concluding with a bleeped expletive. A somber, dramatically lit black-and-white side-profile of Donald Trump is shown as the narrator's voice states, 'We're going to run a country.' The segment concludes with Robert Ray, former Independent Counsel, speaking with a focused, analytical tone in a dimly lit room, discussing how a new president would be cautious if someone came to them with information, stating, 'on the one hand, you might...'

The video transitions to a black-and-white close-up of Robert Mueller, his face partially shadowed, conveying a contemplative and intense mood. Sec. of Homeland Security, a bald man in a dark suit and patterned tie, speaks with a serious, calm, and measured voice in a formal interview setting, reflecting on whether Jim Comey understood the high stakes of a meeting. The segment concludes with a final black-and-white close-up of Robert Mueller, his face in profile, lit dramatically, reinforcing the gravity of the narrative, all set against the persistent dramatic instrumental music.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a dramatic black-and-white close-up of James Comey, a bald man with glasses, walking seriously in a suit and tie, as a male narrator explains that Trump was already distrustful of an FBI director who served under President Obama. This transitions to a close-up of a classified document, marked 'SECRET//ORCON//NOFORN', detailing Comey's notes from a private session with President Trump on January 6, 2017, with key text underlined in red, while the narrator reveals Comey typed this memo in his SUV immediately after the meeting. The narrator continues, recounting Trump's alleged comments about women and a stripper from the memo, as a man in a suit, possibly Comey, exits a vehicle with a briefcase. Matthew Miller, identified as from the Department of Justice (2009–11), then appears in a formal interview setting, his distinct voice heard stating it was unsurprising that the President-elect immediately memorialized the meeting for record-keeping. Exterior shots of the imposing Trump Tower with its golden signage follow, as the narrator describes the President-elect's fury in the aftermath of the meeting. An older interviewee with gray hair and glasses, wearing a suit and red tie, then speaks, his distinct voice explaining that Trump viewed the situation as 'blackmail' and a 'shakedown,' believing Comey was trying to show he 'got something on him.' The segment concludes with a dramatic black-and-white close-up of Donald Trump, his hand thoughtfully on his chin, illuminated by stark lighting, as the interviewee's analysis fades, all underscored by a subtle, suspenseful background score.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of Sebastian Gorka, identified as a Trump adviser, speaking with a serious expression and gesturing as he discusses politically appointed individuals potentially using intelligence as a weapon against those they disagree with. J.D. Gordon, Director of National Security for the Trump campaign, then speaks directly to the camera with a composed demeanor, explaining that when an FBI director approaches a president-elect with "salacious and unverified" information, Trump recognized it as a "shakedown" due to his business background. A distinct news jingle introduces a CNN news broadcast, where an anchor delivers "BREAKING NEWS" with a red and white graphic, reporting that top intelligence officials provided information to President-elect Donald Trump. This coincides with a dramatically lit, black-and-white close-up of Trump's intense face. A voiceover reveals Trump's fear that the story would leak, which it soon did on cable news, leading to an MSNBC screen displaying a BuzzFeed News headline alleging 'Trump Has Deep Ties To Russia,' with a photo of Trump and a note that the report is unverified. Another voiceover details claims by Russian operatives of having compromising personal and financial information about Mr. Trump. The scene then cuts to a Fox News report from Trump Tower, New York City, where a reporter outside discusses BuzzFeed's "controversial move" of publishing a dossier. A voiceover notes that "before long, the entire dossier was on the web," as the scene transitions to a Bloomberg news anchor reporting that 'Trump said to be told of unverified Russian intelligence,' with financial data scrolling on the screen, while another voiceover adds that these details have been "detailed by numerous media outlets." The sequence uses a mix of formal interviews, news broadcasts, and dramatic visuals to build a narrative around unverified intelligence and political tension surrounding Trump’s ties to Russia.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a wide shot of a press conference, likely within Trump Tower, where Donald Trump stands at a podium labeled 'THE OFFICE OF THE PRESIDENT ELECT,' flanked by American flags. The audio begins with a voice listing media outlets like 'BuzzFeed, The New York Times, and CNN,' followed by a narrator stating, 'The next day, Trump went before the cameras to fight back.' John Cassidy from The New Yorker then appears in a close-up interview, speaking in a dimly lit setting, expressing surprise at how 'vituperatively and so angrily' Trump came out. The scene returns to Trump, wearing a dark suit and red tie, speaking passionately and gesturing with his right hand, his voice angry as he calls the actions of intelligence agencies 'disgraceful' for allowing 'false and fake' information out. J.D. Gordon, a Trump campaign adviser, is then shown in a formal interview setting, explaining that Trump 'expresses frustration' and 'knows it's a setup,' a 'plot to destroy him.' Back at the podium, Trump continues his emphatic speech, comparing the situation to 'Nazi Germany' and calling it a 'disgrace.' A man with gray hair and glasses, in an animated interview, further elaborates, suggesting it's 'something the Gestapo would have done,' before a narrator interjects, 'Trump says.' Trump is again seen at the podium, reiterating that 'information that was false and fake and never happened got released to the public.' The final shots show the audience of journalists actively taking notes and recording, highlighting the intense media coverage, while John Cassidy's voice returns, stating that Trump 'now viewed the entire intelligence community and the FBI as enemies.' The segment concludes with faint audience chatter and a journalist asking for a question.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with Donald Trump, dressed in a dark suit, white shirt, and red tie with an American flag pin, speaking passionately and gesturing emphatically from a podium labeled 'THE OFFICE OF THE PRESIDENT ELECT, New York, New York,' surrounded by multiple American flags against a blue backdrop. His voice is heard, punctuated by the clamor of reporters from the audience, who are seen taking notes and using phones, as they repeatedly ask questions like "Can you give us a chance?" and accuse him of "attacking our news organization," to which he or others respond with "Don't be." On-screen text credits the production team, including 'PRODUCED BY MICHAEL KIRK, MIKE WISER, PHILIP BENNETT,' 'PRODUCED & REPORTED BY JIM GILMORE, GABRIELLE SCHONDER,' 'WRITTEN BY MICHAEL KIRK & MIKE WISER,' and 'DIRECTED BY MICHAEL KIRK.' A dramatic, suspenseful score begins as the scene transitions to a black-and-white sequence with bold red text proclaiming 'TRUMP'S SHOWDOWN,' while a narrator's calm, authoritative voice explains that Trump's "combative strategy" would define his first years in office and lead to a "showdown with a special counsel" that now threatens his presidency. This narrative is accompanied by grainy black-and-white footage of a man in a suit, possibly a historical figure, followed by a close-up of another man in a suit in a dimly lit, high-contrast setting, as the narrator continues, stating that Trump's "combative nature" had developed decades earlier when he learned his method for attack and confrontation.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with grainy black-and-white archival footage of a man, identified by a male narrator as "notorious lawyer and fixer Roy Cohn," speaking intensely to another man in a dark suit who smiles and listens, while a third man briefly joins. The scene transitions to a modern interview setting where Marie Brenner from Vanity Fair, seen speaking expressively with hand gestures, wearing glasses and a dark sleeveless top, states that "Trump was created by the politics of intimidation taught to him by his mentor Roy Cohn," describing Cohn as "his alter ego," "confidant," and "ersatz father." On-screen text identifies her as "MARIE BRENNER, Vanity Fair" and displays the "FRONTLINE" logo. A close-up of a mechanical device, possibly a film projector or camera, with a glowing light and moving parts, is shown. This is followed by archival black-and-white footage of the U.S. Capitol Building, bustling with cars and people, as the narrator explains that "Cohn had become a national figure in the 1950s." Dramatic, old-timey music begins, accompanying a shift to a crowded hearing room filled with men in suits, microphones, and documents. An archival announcer's voice states, "The Red-hunting Senator Joseph McCarthy attends," and the narrator adds, "during a different showdown in Washington." Typewriter and siren-like sounds are heard as the archival announcer continues, "The scene is Washington and a Senate investigating subcommittee. Mr. Cohn, his friend and aide, was present with Senator McCarthy." The narrator then refers to "the McCarthy hearings, an investigation to expose American communists," calling it "a witch hunt." The camera focuses on individuals at the table, including a man with glasses and another with a serious expression, while another archival voice describes McCarthy "ripping into the reputations of both friend and foe alike." The segment ends on a close-up of a man in a suit looking intently off-camera, as the narrator concludes, "Helping to run the show, Roy Cohn," and the music fades.

The scene transitions to a modern interview setting with Marie Brenner from Vanity Fair, who speaks expressively with hand gestures, wearing glasses and a dark sleeveless top. segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Marie Brenner, a woman with short blonde hair, tortoiseshell glasses, and a dark sleeveless turtleneck, speaking animatedly in a modern interview setting. Her clear, articulate voice introduces Roy Cohn as an 'architect of the most sinister period in American history.' The scene then transitions to grainy black-and-white archival footage of a crowded hearing room, where a formal male voice is heard presenting 'detailed testimony' regarding 'Julius Rosenberg.' A male narrator's voice then describes Cohn's tactics to 'root out communists deep inside the government,' as the visuals show intense close-ups of two men in suits, one with a pen, in high-contrast lighting, emphasizing the gravity of their exchange. Marie Brenner returns, visually and audibly, elaborating on Cohn's destructive methods, stating 'they smeared people as communists,' 'made up charges,' and 'ruined lives,' even driving some 'to suicide,' adding that Cohn 'never felt guilty for any of it.' The archival footage resumes with a close-up of two men, one covering his mouth, suggesting concern. The narrator's voice continues, detailing Cohn's downfall and return to New York City, coinciding with a visual of a man reading a 'DAILY NEWS' newspaper with the headline 'COHN RESIGNS M’CARTHY POST.' The final shot shows a man in a light suit walking beside a vintage car with a New Jersey license plate reading 'ROY C,' reinforcing the historical context as the narrator describes Cohn's reputation as a 'ruthless attack dog and political' figure before the audio cuts off.

The segment opens with a close-up on a legal document, highlighting the phrase 'into a gestapo-like interrogation' underlined in red, as a narrator explains that in court filings, Roy Cohn compared the Department of Justice to the Nazis, alleging such tactics. Frank Rich of New York Magazine, speaking in a dimly lit interview, describes this as a consistent pattern of attacking accusers and the Justice Department to create a smoke screen around the original crime. The scene transitions to a signed legal document featuring signatures from Roy M. Cohn, Fred C. Trump, and Donald Trump for the defendants, alongside the Department of Justice for the plaintiff, as the narrator reveals Cohn's suit was thrown out and Trump was forced to settle, having lost. However, a black-and-white archival clip of an intently speaking man is shown while the narrator notes that Trump and Cohn declared victory in the press. Elyse Goldweber, a former Justice Department lawyer, then comments in a modern interview, gesturing that the facts were irrelevant, as it was presented as a victory of 'beating the government,' which 'had nothing to do with reality.' Following a black-and-white archival clip of a younger, intense Donald Trump, Tony Schwartz, co-author of 'The Art of the Deal,' animatedly explains Trump as a 'counter-puncher' who 'admits nothing,' never acknowledges mistakes, and always declares victory even in defeat.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a man in a blue blazer and white shirt, speaking animatedly with expressive hand gestures, describing someone who "lost as clearly as you can lose, but he loudly proclaimed his victory." This commentary continues over black-and-white archival footage showing two men in formal attire, one possibly Roy Cohn, engaged in a serious conversation at a social event with balloons, as the narrator explains that "Cohn had given Trump a formula for survival and success in lawsuits and life," which "Trump adopted." The scene transitions to grainy color footage of Donald Trump, in a suit and red tie, surrounded by reporters and cameras in a crowded area, with audible press scrum noise. Trump's voice is heard saying, "I haven't gone to...", "I don't like...", and more clearly, "You can't say that I give up very easily," and later, "Not at all, we had a great victory. I'm happy as hell. Thank you very much." The narrator adds that Trump would "rely on what Roy Cohn taught him" through "more than 4,000 lawsuits." The segment then cuts to Adam Leitman Bailey, a bald man in a dark pinstripe suit and red tie, identified by on-screen text as 'ADAM LEITMAN BAILEY, New York attorney,' speaking directly to the camera in a professional setting. He states, "In the old days, the sport of kings and queens was horse racing. Now, it's litigation. And Donald Trump definitely plays that sport. And he plays it very well." The video concludes with an aerial view of the White House, surrounded by green lawns, with the Washington Monument visible in the distance under overcast skies, as the narrator mentions "a brand new team in charge of the White House," accompanied by dramatic, investigative-style background music and the PBS and FRONTLINE logos.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with an aerial view of the White House, its grand white facade and manicured lawns visible with the Washington Monument in the distance under overcast skies, displaying the FRONTLINE logo, as a news reporter announces, "President Trump wakes up with a busy day ahead." A female voice adds, "This is just the beginning of what it's going to be." Dramatic, suspenseful music begins to play as the scene transitions to black-and-white footage of Donald Trump walking through a dimly lit room and then exiting a vehicle, conveying a somber, dramatic tone. A deep, authoritative narrator's voice states, "As President, Donald Trump came to Washington determined to confront the established order," while a motorcade, including a black SUV with flashing lights and police motorcycles, moves through a city street near a government building, suggesting high-security movement. The narrator continues, "He used Roy Cohn's attack strategy," as a news clip mentions taking "on the powers that be." The video then cuts to an interview with Ben Shapiro of The Daily Wire, who, in a close-up with a dark background, asserts that Trump "is just going to start action right away... he's going to fix everything, he's going to set the world right immediately." Donald Trump is next seen in a crowded outdoor press scrum, wearing a dark suit and red tie, surrounded by reporters and cameras, gesturing as he speaks. The scene shifts to Trump seated at a desk inside an aircraft, wearing a white shirt and striped tie, signing documents while being filmed. The narrator describes Trump's approach as a "brawl with critics and the media," with news clips mentioning "falsely accusing the press" and the need to "sort out friend from foe." This is followed by an interview with Corey Lewandowski, former Trump Campaign Manager, who, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit setting, characterizes Trump as "a man always in motion. He always wants things. But you know what? People aren't used to that. People aren't used to a president who's going directly to the American people with his Twitter feed." Trump is then shown walking through a grand hallway with chandeliers, accompanied by others, before returning to an outdoor press event where he is again surrounded by media, pointing and speaking animatedly. News clips report "a wild day at the Trump White House" and "President Trump's travel ban has been blocked in the courts," alongside mentions of "executive actions, tweets, firings," and "a lot of chaos and controversy here in Washington D.C." The narrator concludes, "In Washington, it was seen as chaos. But this was Trump's comfort zone," as the final shot captures a chaotic indoor scene with Trump in a crowded room, surrounded by people holding phones and cameras, capturing a tense, high-energy moment, with the dramatic music swelling to a peak.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a chaotic, close-up shot of Donald Trump navigating a dense press scrum, surrounded by reporters and cameras, as a male voice, identified as Trump campaign figure David Urban, discusses the bureaucracy struggling to keep up with a rapid pace, creating a sense of 'whiplash.' The scene transitions to a formal interview with Urban, wearing a dark suit and striped tie, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit setting. This is followed by an interview with Chris Whipple, author of 'The Gatekeepers,' who, in a dark setting, details a 'looming showdown with the FBI and James Comey,' noting they would 'face off again at a photo op.' Whipple describes 'Trump's dislike for James Comey' as 'visceral,' explaining that Comey's investigation into 'Russian collusion' made Trump 'extremely nervous,' partly due to the 'infamous briefing about the dossier' that 'drove Trump crazy.' The footage then cuts to a grand, ornate room, likely within the White House, where Trump, in a dark suit, walks through a corridor lined with uniformed Secret Service agents and staff, entering a large, circular room with blue carpeting, chandeliers, and portraits. He is greeted by applause and handshakes from a group of men in suits and uniforms, including a man with gold epaulets. During this, Benjamin Wittes, identified as a 'Friend of James Comey,' describes Comey 'standing in a blue blazer against blue drapes,' emphasizing it 'was not an accident' and that Comey was 'literally as far away from Trump as it is possible to be in that room.' The video shifts to black-and-white archival footage of Trump in the same room, speaking to a group, with a dramatic silhouette shot, possibly of Comey, adding tension. Whipple's voice returns, stating, 'This time, Trump made it clear who was in charge,' followed by Wittes recounting how 'the President calls him out and says, 'No, no, no, come here, come here,'' amidst sounds of a microphone adjustment and indistinct speech. The segment concludes with Trump, seen from behind, addressing the assembled group in the opulent room, gesturing with his right hand, as the decor remains prominent.

The footage then cuts to a grand, ornate room—likely within the White House—where Trump, in a dark suit, walks through a corridor lined with uniformed Secret Service agents and staff, entering a large, circular room with blue carpeting, chandeliers, and portraits. He is greeted by applause and handshakes from a group of men in suits and uniforms, including a man in a dark uniform with gold epaulets and a badge. segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with Carrie Johnson from NPR, in a blue blazer, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit interview setting. The scene transitions to an opulent White House room, where Donald Trump, in a dark suit and red tie, is seen shaking hands with a man in a blue suit and pink tie, surrounded by Secret Service agents in dark uniforms with gold epaulets and other officials. The grand room features ornate portraits, chandeliers, and a large blue carpet with a circular pattern. An unseen speaker's voice describes this interaction, noting how 'this guy's more famous than me,' and that 'Comey knows even then, it's going to be a problem.' The speaker details the man's attempt to maintain distance with an outstretched arm for 'just a nice, distant handshake,' but instead, 'the President pulls him in' and 'goes for the hug.' The speaker questions, 'Why is it unclear exactly why, but goes in for the hug.' Another voice adds, 'Trump pulls him in, and whispers something in his ear,' observing that 'to the camera, it looks as if Trump is giving him a kiss on the cheek.' The audio emphasizes that 'when somebody's the President, the least tiny gesture is magnified by 12 billion times,' explaining that pulling someone over, whispering, or hugging them is 'a big deal.' The camera follows Trump engaging with attendees, with photographers capturing the moment. The footage cuts to Carol Leonnig from The Washington Post speaking in a similar dimly lit interview setting, followed by a dramatic black-and-white close-up of Trump and the man in the blue suit shaking hands, emphasizing the moment’s significance. Gwenda Blair, author of 'The Trumps,' then speaks from a softly lit room with bookshelves, wearing a vibrant pink blouse. The video returns to the White House setting, showing Trump gesturing as he addresses a group of officials and security personnel amidst the grand room’s chandeliers and large windows. The audio concludes with a voice stating, 'Comey sees danger, Comey sees politics, Comey doesn't want any part of this,' attributing the situation to 'pushing Jim Comey to come across.' Trump turns to face the group, continuing his address, while the camera captures the formal atmosphere and the presence of photographers and onlookers.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Peter Baker, identified as 'Co-Author, Kremlin Rising,' speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit, warm-toned setting. He explains, in a clear, authoritative tone, how someone was "setting the tone of their relationship," demanding loyalty: "You work for me. Uh, you're loyal to me, right? That's what he wants to know. He wants to know you're loyal to me." A narrator's voice then states that "For James Comey, Donald Trump was a threat to the FBI's independence," and "a threat to the reputation he had built over a lifelong career in law enforcement." During this narration, the scene dramatically shifts to high-contrast black-and-white close-ups of a man in a suit, his face partially shadowed, eyes wide and mouth slightly open, conveying intensity or distress, followed by another man looking upward with a serious expression. The narrator continues, mentioning that "He had begun as an Assistant United States Attorney." The video then cuts to Nelson Cunningham, 'Fmr. Asst. U.S. Attorney,' who, in a measured, experienced tone, recounts meeting Jim Comey "30 years ago when he and I both started as Assistant US Attorneys in the Southern District of New York," adding that "Rudy Giuliani swore us in a few weeks apart." Cunningham, speaking seriously and gesturing in a dimly lit setting, describes Comey as a young prosecutor who was "straight down the middle, uh, always turning square corners." The 'FRONTLINE' logo is consistently displayed in the lower left during these interview segments. The narrator's voice returns, stating that "Under George W. Bush, he was named Deputy Attorney General," but "clashed with the president when he led a revolt of Justice Department officials." The segment concludes with Peter Baker's voice, stating, "His real claim to fame is this..." The alternating interview and stark black-and-white shots, combined with the serious dialogue and narration, create a tense, investigative atmosphere throughout.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Matt Apuzzo, identified as from The New York Times, speaking directly to the camera in a warmly lit room, his clear voice discussing a moment during the Bush administration when someone refused to support a provision of President Bush's warrantless wiretapping program, leading to the White House being "incensed." A black-and-white close-up then shows a man in a dark suit and patterned tie walking in profile with a serious expression, followed by Jack Goldsmith, identified as a Fmr. Asst. Attorney General, speaking animatedly in a dimly lit interview. Goldsmith's earnest voice explains that the individual felt strongly about the Justice Department's job to uphold the law, taking it "really seriously." The scene transitions to an exterior shot of the White House under overcast skies, with a fountain in the foreground, as a narrator's voice explains that the White House "backed down" after Comey and other Justice Department officials threatened to resign. Matt Apuzzo reappears, stating that what happened next reveals a lot about Comey, specifically that Jim Comey ultimately "testified in a major way before Congress." Archival footage then shows a man in a dark suit and checkered tie, presumably Jim Comey, speaking at a microphone in a formal hearing room, surrounded by other attendees, some with laptops. His firm voice declares he "couldn't stay" if the administration engaged in conduct without legal basis. The camera briefly cuts to an intently listening audience member before returning to the speaker. Another male voice, seemingly an interrogator, commends "Mr. Comey" for his actions, before the narrator concludes that this was one of many times Comey would go before Congress, all underscored by subtle, dramatic background music.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of a man in a dark suit and checkered tie speaking at a microphone in a formal hearing room, surrounded by attendees. The scene transitions to an interview with Bob Anderson, identified as 'Fmr. FBI Exec. Asst. Director,' who, in a dimly lit setting, states that the subject was 'in the spotlight more than any FBI director that I ever worked for' and 'felt that he could use that spotlight in ways that other directors hadn't used.' A male voice then adds, 'having the courage to speak the truth,' followed by the sound of applause and a brief 'Yeah' from the hearing room. The footage cuts back to the hearing room, showing a man with glasses and a striped tie leaning forward, appearing engaged, then a wide shot of the crowded room. A man in a dark suit stands and walks away, with others following, as the applause continues. The scene shifts to an interview with a woman in a blue blazer, who, speaking directly to the camera, explains that 'Republicans who don't like Comey because of his steps in the George W. Bush administration tend to call him Saint Jim, a guy in love with his own rectitude.' The video returns to the hearing room with attendees rising and moving, including the man in the dark suit and checkered tie walking through the crowd. Next, Robert Ray, 'Fmr. Independent Counsel,' is seen in a dimly lit interview, asserting that 'Jim Comey is by his own acknowledgement has a very black and white view of the world with very little room for gray,' adding that 'if he decides that it's the right thing to do, everybody else should agree with him.' A news broadcast sounder then plays, with a voice confirming 'FBI Director James Comey' and another voice stating, 'President Barack Obama named Comey FBI Director.' The news sounder continues as the scene transitions to an exterior shot of the J. Edgar Hoover FBI Building with American flags waving. The segment concludes with a man in a dark suit and yellow tie walking to a podium in a room adorned with American and Department of Justice flags, preparing to speak, as another man in a suit enters behind him, while the audio discusses Comey making himself 'the face of the FBI's investigation of Hillary Clinton' during the 2016 campaign.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a man in a dark suit and gold tie at a podium, flanked by Department of Justice flags, speaking formally about individuals being "extremely careless in their handling of very sensitive, highly classified information." The scene transitions to a close-up of a man with glasses in a dark suit and patterned tie, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit, warm-toned setting, explaining that "Comey has been at the middle of this election and arguably is the person who most significantly influenced it other than the candidates themselves." A dramatic black-and-white sequence then shows a man in a suit, his face illuminated from below, gazing upward with a serious expression. This is followed by 'MATTHEW MILLER, Department of Justice, 2009–11,' speaking in a formal interview setting, asserting that "There's a through line in Jim Comey's career, and that is this intense conviction that he is sometimes the only righteous person in any organization," and that his "self-regard... can cross into self-righteousness." A black-and-white shot of a man in a suit walking through a dimly lit corridor with silhouetted figures adds tension. Finally, a man with a beard and graying hair, wearing a dark blazer over a checkered shirt, speaks directly to the camera in a warmly lit room, stating that "Russia has come up again and again," and that as "Comey led the investigation into Russian interference, he would find himself on a collision course with the President," adding that "The very inauguration of President Trump... poses challenges to the FBI because they have investigations on..." The segment concludes with a black-and-white close-up of a man's shadowed face, conveying intensity. The video uses shifts in lighting, color, and camera angles to build a narrative of official proceedings, personal reflection, and dramatic tension.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a dramatic black-and-white sequence, featuring a man in a suit and tie in a high-contrast close-up, followed by another man in a suit engaged in a tense conversation, and a third with a stern expression. Over these intense visuals, a male voice lists individuals under FBI investigation: Paul Manafort, Carter Page, George Papadopoulos, and Michael Flynn, the National Security Advisor, all described as being in the national security space. The tone shifts to color as a man with graying hair and a beard, wearing a dark blazer over a checkered shirt, speaks directly to the camera in a warmly lit room, as another male voice mentions "players like Russia." Tense, investigative music underscores the entire segment. An exterior shot of a black van with wooden lattice structures is seen near the imposing J. Edgar Hoover FBI Building, as a narrator states that Comey dispatched two FBI agents to the White House on a sensitive mission. A man with glasses and dark hair, wearing a dark suit, then speaks in a dimly lit setting, explaining that the agents went to the White House to interview the National Security Advisor just days after a new administration began, an act he considers unprecedented. Exterior views of the White House under overcast skies and a grand, classical-style building with a portico follow, with people walking nearby. A close-up of a dark door with a gold handle and number '725' is shown. Another man with glasses and graying hair, wearing a dark suit and blue polka-dot tie, speaks seriously in a dimly lit room, questioning the FBI's presence at the White House and Michael Flynn's meeting with agents without a lawyer, asserting that such an event should never happen. The final frames return to black-and-white, showing a man with a patterned tie, arms crossed, then a close-up of his face as he touches it, conveying contemplation and concern over the unfolding events.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a dramatic black-and-white close-up of an older man, his face etched with deep lines, hand pressed to his mouth, as a male narrator with a deep, serious tone explains the FBI's interest in a phone call Flynn had with Russian Ambassador Sergey Kislyak during the transition. The scene transitions to Karen DeYoung, identified by on-screen text as 'The Washington Post' under the 'FRONTLINE' logo, speaking directly to the camera with a clear, articulate voice, detailing how Kislyak initiated a call to Flynn, who then got word and eventually spoke with him later that day. DeYoung poses the central question, "The question is what was said," before the video cuts to an exterior rooftop shot at dusk, showing satellite dishes and a distant Washington Monument, with a plane flying overhead, establishing a Washington D.C. setting. The narrator continues, revealing the FBI already knew from electronic surveillance that Flynn and Kislyak had discussed the Obama administration's sanctions on Russia, and that "on the record, they asked Flynn about it." A stark black-and-white close-up of a man with short, graying hair and intense eyes appears, looking directly at the camera, as Michael Isikoff, identified by on-screen text as 'Co-Author, Russian Roulette', speaks calmly and analytically, describing how Flynn "dissembles," suggesting he did not have such conversations with the Russian ambassador. The narrator then states, "Court documents detailed what happened," leading to a close-up of a legal document with red underlines, where an authoritative male voice reads the highlighted text, accusing Flynn of falsely stating he did not ask Russia’s ambassador to refrain from escalating tensions in response to U.S. sanctions. The scene returns to the black-and-white close-up of the man with graying hair, now looking downward with a somber expression, as the narrator declares, "Comey's agents had caught Flynn lying to the FBI. A federal crime." A brief, news reporter-style female voice interjects, raising "new questions about coincidental timing of a call," before the segment concludes with an exterior view of the imposing J. Edgar Hoover FBI Building on a gray day, with cars moving along the street, as the narrator's voice fades out, stating, "At FBI headquarters..."

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a wide, moving shot of a city street, likely Washington D.C., featuring the imposing J. Edgar Hoover FBI Building on a gray, overcast day, with cars and traffic lights, establishing a formal, institutional atmosphere. A male narrator begins, explaining that information Comey had was "explosive," and evidence against Flynn was shared with the Justice Department, leading Acting Attorney General Sally Yates to be "read in" to Flynn's FBI interview. The scene transitions to a series of black-and-white close-ups of individuals with intense, serious expressions, interspersed with color shots. Carrie Johnson from NPR, seen in a blue blazer, describes Sally Yates as a "career Justice Department prosecutor, 27 years in the department, a tough cookie, but a genteel Southern woman." She continues, detailing Yates's stance: "She's saying, we need to tell the White House. Flynn has lied. And the Russians know that those are lies. We need to mitigate the risk." The male narrator then states Yates would meet with White House counsel Don McGahn. Emily Bazelon, a Lecturer at Yale Law School, appears in a red top, using animated hand gestures as she describes Don McGahn as a "stalwart conservative lawyer," not someone "schooled in exactly these kinds of questions." The male narrator adds that Yates sent her deputy, Mary McCord, to inform McGahn about Michael Flynn's lying. Mary McCord, identified as a former Acting Assistant Attorney General and seen in a gray blazer, speaks with a composed, authoritative tone, reflecting that for someone in office for only six days, the information was "surprising." The video concludes with a dramatic black-and-white close-up of a man with a stern, partially shadowed face, emphasizing gravity and tension throughout the narrative focused on legal and investigative themes, characteristic of a FRONTLINE documentary.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a dramatic black-and-white close-up of a man’s face, partially shadowed, conveying intensity and contemplation. This transitions to a color interview with Jeh Johnson, identified as 'Fmr. Sec. of Homeland Security,' who speaks in a calm, authoritative male voice about someone "compromised" within the West Wing. A black-and-white shot of a man in deep thought, hand on chin, suggests serious reflection, as a male narrator explains that information given to McGann was intended to force the president to act. Carol Leonnig of The Washington Post then appears, speaking directly to the camera in a clear, analytical female voice, stating that Yates and McCord believed it was a "very vulnerable situation" for the White House and Mr. Flynn, presuming he would be fired. The scene returns to black-and-white, showing a man in a suit listening intently to another man, followed by a stern-faced man, as the narrator continues, detailing how McGann informed the president that the Justice Department believed Flynn had been lying, a warning that a trusted advisor was in the crosshairs. Michael Isikoff, co-author of 'Russian Roulette,' then speaks in an analytical male voice, identifying Michael Flynn as the former director of the Defense Intelligence Agency and Trump's top national security advisor. The segment concludes with a wide, vibrant shot of the 2016 Republican Convention, where an enthusiastic female announcer introduces "retired United States Army Lieutenant General Michael Flynn" to a massive, cheering crowd, as a man in a dark suit walks confidently toward a podium.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a wide shot of the Republican Convention, 2016, as Lt. Gen. Michael T. Flynn, Ret., dressed in a dark suit and red-and-white striped tie, walks confidently toward a modern, illuminated podium on a grand stage. As he begins speaking, J.D. Gordon, a Trump campaign adviser, appears in a dimly lit interview, stating that Flynn "became a very trusted and close confidant of not just President Trump, but President Trump's family." The scene returns to Flynn, delivering a passionate speech with emphatic hand gestures, his face animated and intense, declaring, "We do not need a reckless president who believes she is above the law!" as the crowd cheers and chants "Lock her up!" The stage backdrop displays his image and the slogan 'MAKE AMERICA SAFE AGAIN'. A narrator explains that "Flynn's harsh rhetoric had endeared him to Trump and his supporters" and that "Trump and Flynn bonded." The video transitions to black-and-white footage of Flynn speaking earnestly, then to Sebastian Gorka, a former Trump adviser, in a formal interview, who remarks on the "very good chemistry" and "personal chemistry" between General Flynn and the President. Ominous music begins as the narrator reveals that "Trump had been told Flynn was in trouble with the FBI." Black-and-white archival footage shows Donald Trump in profile beside Flynn. Peter Baker of The New York Times then appears in a dimly lit interview, stating that "Mike Flynn knows a lot of things" and "could be a dangerous person to have under pursuit of investigators," noting the investigation's potential to get "out of his control." The final shot is a black-and-white close-up of Flynn, dramatically illuminated, speaking intensely into a microphone.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a black-and-white archival shot of Donald Trump, serious and focused, seated at a desk with a telephone receiver to his ear, as an unidentified male narrator explains that one day after discussing Flynn with McGann, Trump took a fateful step by calling Comey. This transitions to a color interview with Carrie Johnson of NPR, wearing a blue blazer, who recounts Trump's 'surprise call' inviting Comey for dinner. A dramatic black-and-white close-up of Robert Mueller, partially shadowed, conveys intensity. Over a visual of an unidentified man in a dark suit and purple tie speaking seriously, Matt Apuzzo's voice from The New York Times clarifies that the dinner invitation was 'clearly not a coincidence' given the briefing about Mike Flynn's 'criminal jeopardy.' The scene then cuts to a black-and-white image of James Comey, smiling slightly while walking, as the narrator continues, detailing Comey's arrival to find the table set for two. Carrie Johnson reappears, speaking with expressive hand gestures, emphasizing the intimate setting and the 'attempted bromance.' A close-up of FBI documents, marked 'CONFIDENTIAL/NOFORN,' detailing the dinner between President Trump and James Comey, is shown as the narrator states Comey 'memorialized the conversation.' Matt Apuzzo's voice then reads from the document, describing the setting: 'We sat facing each other at a small oval table set for two and placed in the center of the room.' The video then shows Matt Apuzzo in a warmly lit interview setting, continuing to explain that Comey noted the president had 'very nice words for him,' making it a 'pleasant conversation,' before the audio cuts off with 'And then the president's...' The final shot returns to the FBI document, highlighting in red the critical line: 'He replied that he needed loyalty and expected loyalty.'

The scene transitions to a color interview with Carrie Johnson, identified by on-screen text as 'Carrie Johnson, NPR,' wearing a blue blazer and speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit setting. The video then cuts to a dramatic black-and-white close-up of Robert Mueller, his face partially shadowed, conveying intensity and contemplation. This is followed by a color interview with a man in a dark suit and purple tie, who appears to be speaking with a serious expression. The sequence returns to a black-and-white image of James Comey, smiling slightly while walking, then cuts back to Carrie Johnson speaking with expressive hand gestures. The video then shows a close-up of FBI documents, marked 'CONFIDENTIAL/NOFORN,' with text describing a dinner between President Trump and James Comey in the White House Green Room, including details about the conversation and servers. The scene shifts to an interview with Matt Apuzzo, identified as 'Matt Apuzzo, The New York Times,' wearing a dark blazer over a patterned shirt, speaking directly to the camera in a warmly lit room. The final shot returns to the FBI document, highlighting the line 'He replied that he needed loyalty and expected loyalty,' underlined in red, emphasizing the gravity of the statement.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a typed document, highlighting a red-underlined passage. A male voiceover reads the text: 'He replied that he needed loyalty and expected loyalty. I did not reply, or even change my facial expression.' The camera then transitions to a series of black-and-white close-ups of Donald Trump, shown in various formal settings—seated, speaking, and looking pensive—emphasizing a serious, introspective mood. The footage alternates between these archival-style shots and color interview segments. A man with gray hair and glasses, wearing a dark suit and patterned tie, speaks directly to the camera in a dimly lit, warm-toned setting, stating, 'The President would ask for Comey's loyalty several times during the dinner,' and elaborating on the 'remarkable moment' of a president demanding loyalty from an FBI director, which is 'not the role.' The male voiceover returns, reading more text: 'He then returned to loyalty, saying, 'I need loyalty.' I replied that he would always get honesty from me.' Robert Costa of The Washington Post, identified by on-screen text and appearing in a formal interview setup, then explains that 'President Trump was feeling out Comey about where the investigation stood,' and that 'Comey saw it as intimidation, possible obstruction of justice. This is the moment where things really start to split.' The sequence continues with Michael Isikoff, Co-Author of 'Russian Roulette,' identified by on-screen text and speaking with expressive hand gestures in a dimly lit room, asserting, 'This is the Roy Cohn view of the world, this has been the Donald Trump view of the world. This is the way he's done business, either you're with me or you're against me.' The visual rhythm alternates between the somber, monochromatic Trump footage and the more polished, color interview segments, creating an overall atmosphere of gravity and investigative depth, focusing on political loyalty, power dynamics, and media scrutiny.

This is followed by Robert Costa of The Washington Post, identified by on-screen text, who appears in a similar formal interview setup. The sequence continues with another man, identified as Michael Isikoff, Co-Author of 'Russian Roulette,' speaking with expressive hand gestures in a dimly lit room. The visual rhythm alternates between the somber, monochromatic Trump footage and the more polished, color interview segments, creating a contrast between historical context and contemporary analysis. The overall atmosphere is one of gravity and investigative depth, focusing on political loyalty, power dynamics, and media scrutiny.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a black-and-white close-up of Donald Trump in profile, appearing serious, as a male voice asks, "Are you on my team or are you on the other guy's team?" and then states, "Trump had failed to win Comey over." The scene transitions to the rhythmic whirring and clatter of a printing press in motion, followed by a close-up of The Washington Post newspaper dated February 10, 2017, with headlines about an immigration ban and Russia discussions. A male narrator explains, "Then, two weeks later, there was another leak. This one to The Washington Post." Karen DeYoung, identified as from The Washington Post, appears on screen, her voice heard stating, "Washington Post broke this news. They say that National Security Advisor Michael Flynn did..." The narrator interjects, detailing how "The Post scoop publicly revealed the details of that electronic surveillance of Flynn's call with Russian Ambassador Kislyak." DeYoung then elaborates, "We had found out that in fact there were intercepts and had a variety of sources saying that yes, they had discussed sanctions." As a reporter's voice mentions "multiple news outlets report," the video shifts to an outdoor tarmac where Donald Trump, in a dark suit and red tie, exits a 'UNITED STATES OF AMERICA' helicopter, accompanied by staff and military personnel. Melania Trump, in a dark coat and white pants, walks towards him, smiling as she enters an aircraft, with Trump following. The narrator explains, "As the story dominated the news coverage in Washington, the President headed to Florida, accompanied by the Prime Minister of Japan and Mike Flynn." Another reporter adds, "that goes against the denials of Flynn himself." Inside the aircraft doorway, with a 'PRESIDENTIAL CABIN' display visible, a female reporter's voice asks Trump about "conversations with the Russians about sanctions before you were sworn in," to which Trump, with a serious expression, responds, "I don't know, I haven't seen it," while Melania looks on neutrally.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with Donald Trump, in a dark suit and red patterned tie, and Melania Trump, in a dark coat, standing in the doorway of an aircraft identified as the 'PRESIDENTIAL CABIN' with the time '3:46'. Trump, with a serious expression, audibly responds to an off-screen question about a report, stating, "I don't know about it. I haven't seen it," and "What report is that? I haven't seen that, I'll look at that," before adding, "Thank you. Thank you very much." A narrator interjects that "though he had known about the intercepts for weeks, Trump claimed ignorance." They then exit the aircraft, greeted by a woman, and a motorcade of black SUVs and a limousine, including a Suburban with an American flag, drives away on the tarmac under a dusky sky, with the 'FRONTLINE' logo visible. The scene transitions to a montage of black-and-white 'The Washington Post' headlines from February 10, 2017, detailing allegations that Michael Flynn discussed U.S. sanctions with the Russian ambassador, with headlines like 'Flynn, Russian Envoy Discussed Sanctions' and 'Trump to Look Into Reports on Flynn'. A female news anchor's voice is heard discussing how former acting Attorney General Sally Yates informed the Trump White House about Flynn, and a narrator adds that the meeting between Yates and White House counsel Don McGahn had also leaked, with the news anchor further explaining that the National Security Advisor might be "personally compromised" and "vulnerable to Russian blackmail." The video then cuts to an interview with Matthew Miller, identified as 'Department of Justice, 2009–11,' who, wearing a dark suit and purple tie, audibly explains that when it comes out that "they were warned that he was compromised and that he might have lied to the FBI and they did nothing about it," it becomes a scandal implicating "not just Mike Flynn, but the White House Chief of Staff, the President, and anyone who knew about this warning and failed to take action." The narrator concludes that "a full force of the Washington establishment was turned on Trump," as a black-and-white, ghostly overlay of a man with long hair and a beard is superimposed over Miller’s image, creating a dramatic effect.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens dramatically with a black-and-white, ghostly close-up of a historical figure with long hair and a beard, as John Cassidy from The New Yorker begins to speak, describing a "Bermuda Triangle in Washington" where the FBI, media, White House lawyers, and Justice Department were all pressuring for Flynn's departure. Cassidy, seen in a color interview in a dimly lit room, wearing a dark suit and blue tie, gestures earnestly as he elaborates on this pressure. The scene then cuts to a serious and contemplative Donald Trump in black-and-white, standing in a formal setting with a bookshelf, while a narrator explains that "Washington was turning out to be a very different place than New York" for him. Robert Ray, a former Independent Counsel, then appears in a color interview, speaking with a focused expression about how many New Yorkers coming to Washington found "that's not how it is." This is followed by a stark black-and-white profile shot of a man in a suit with an intense gaze, as the narrator reveals that "Under pressure, Trump gave in. He could keep Flynn no longer." A close-up of Michael T. Flynn's resignation letter, praising Trump and Pence, is shown while multiple reporters announce that "National Security Advisor Michael Flynn has stepped down" and "Trump accepted Flynn's resignation," calling it a "shake-up" after a "tumultuous first month." A black-and-white shot of a man, possibly Flynn, looking upward seriously, accompanies the narrator's statement that "Flynn was gone, but still in jeopardy from an active FBI investigation." The segment concludes with a grave, black-and-white close-up of Donald Trump's focused face, as the narrator states, "Now, the President took an extraordinary step."

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Gwenda Blair, identified by on-screen text as 'Author, The Trumps,' speaking in a softly lit room with bookshelves, wearing a vibrant pink blouse. She calmly states, "On Valentine's Day, 2017, there was a meeting in the Oval Office between the Attorney General and the Director of the FBI, Jim Comey." This transitions to a series of dramatic black-and-white close-ups of Donald Trump, showing intense, contemplative expressions as he looks directly at the camera and then upward, with shadows accentuating his features. A male narrator explains, "As the meeting ended, the President tried to empty the room. He wanted to speak to the FBI Director alone." The mood shifts to a woman in a blue blazer, appearing surprised or concerned with wide eyes, as a female voice adds, "And Attorney General Jeff Sessions kind of lingers. And Comey thinks that's because Sessions knows the President should not be meeting alone with the FBI Director." This is followed by a close-up of a declassified document, with text describing a private meeting in the Oval Office, specifically highlighting and zooming in on the red-underlined phrase: 'The President thanked everyone and said he wanted to speak with me alone.' A different male narrator's calm voice recounts, "The AG lingered momentarily by my chair. But the President thanked him and said he wanted to meet with Jim. He repeated this at least one more time to usher people out." The segment then cuts to a man in a dark suit and purple tie, speaking calmly in a dimly lit setting, before returning to another black-and-white close-up of Donald Trump, this time with a more animated expression, mouth slightly open as if mid-sentence. A third male narrator speculates, "If you're Comey, you're sitting there and thinking, what is it the President needs to say to me that he can't say in front of the Attorney General. Jim Comey's a long-time prosecutor and right away, I suspect his antenna were going up and saying, this is evidence of a guilty mind right here, what he's about to ask me to do." Gwenda Blair's voice briefly returns, saying, "He finally gets to..." before the audio cuts.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with Gwenda Blair, author of 'The Trumps,' speaking in a softly lit room with bookshelves, wearing a vibrant pink blouse, as a narrator discusses two individuals in a room and the 'Michael Flynn issue,' recounting how someone 'began by saying he wanted to talk about Mike Flynn,' and asked, 'can you just kind of ease up on him, he's a really good guy.' This leads to a black-and-white close-up of Donald Trump with intense, contemplative expressions, first looking directly at the camera, then upward, while the recounted dialogue continues with 'I hope you can let this go' and 'I replied by saying, I agree he is a good guy, but said no more.' The scene then highlights a typed document with the phrase 'He is a good guy' underlined in red, visually reinforcing the spoken quote. Carol Leonnig, a reporter from The Washington Post, then appears, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit setting, wearing a black sleeveless top and a red beaded necklace, as a new voice questions whether 'the president [is] asking the FBI director to stop looking at Russian interactions with the campaign' and if 'he [is] trying to shut down a counterintelligence probe.' The video cuts back to a black-and-white close-up of Donald Trump, now with a more animated expression and mouth slightly open, as the speaker continues, stating 'Trump's talking to the director of the FBI about an ongoing investigation by the FBI. And at that point, he's really, from Comey's perspective, crossed the line.' Michael Isikoff, co-author of 'Russian Roulette,' then appears in a dimly lit room, wearing a dark suit and red patterned tie, speaking with a serious demeanor, and is heard stating that this is 'really in direct contravention of policies that have been in place ever since Watergate to not have...' The sequence briefly shows a black-and-white image of a man in a suit, partially obscured by shadows, before Mary McCord, former Acting Assistant Attorney General, is shown speaking with a composed, authoritative tone, wearing a gray blazer, with on-screen text identifying her role, as the audio continues to discuss the implications of such actions. The overall sequence uses alternating interview segments and dramatic black-and-white visuals to build tension and emphasize the political and legal gravity of the topic.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a woman in a dark blazer, her shoulder-length gray hair visible, speaking seriously in a dimly lit room, her clear voice discussing the 'interference by the White House in investigations undertaken by the department or the bureau.' This transitions to a dramatic black-and-white close-up of Donald Trump, his face partially shadowed and contemplative, as Carol Leonnig, identified as 'Carol Leonnig, The Washington Post' under the 'FRONTLINE' logo, appears in a dimly lit setting, wearing a black sleeveless top and a red beaded necklace. Her clear voice narrates how Comey, 'fairly sweaty-palmed,' leaves a meeting, goes to his car, and begins 'typing down the words, the phrases that he can remember the President said,' because he's 'that scared of what this is that has just happened.' A close-up of a declassified document then appears, showing the red-underlined phrase 'The President thanked everyone and said he wanted to speak with me alone,' signed 'JBC 2/14/17,' visually reinforcing the urgency of Comey's documentation. Frank Montoya, Jr., identified as 'Fmr. FBI Special Agent in Charge' under the 'FRONTLINE' logo, then speaks directly to the camera in a dark suit, his serious voice explaining the adage that 'if it happened and you didn't write it down, it didn't happen,' and how the President was 'walking himself down this trail to an investigation.' During his commentary, an exterior low-angle shot of the imposing J. Edgar Hoover FBI Building is shown, followed by a black-and-white close-up of a man in a suit, his face partially obscured by shadow, looking intensely forward. A distinct electronic sound effect marks a transition before a male narrator states, 'Once again, Comey and Trump were at odds. Comey headed to the Justice Department to confront Jeff Sessions about what had just happened.' Frank Montoya, Jr. then returns, quoting Comey's urgent words to the Attorney General: 'Comey pulls the Attorney General aside and says, you can't leave me.'
