segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a dramatic black-and-white close-up of a man in a suit, his face partially shadowed, conveying a serious and intense mood, while a male voice (later identified as Matt Apuzzo) discusses the impropriety of being alone with the president and the need for backup. The scene transitions to a color interview with Matt Apuzzo from The New York Times, who, speaking directly to the camera in a warmly lit room, elaborates that the Attorney General's job is to insulate the FBI from inappropriate political interference, questioning if "Sessions realized that he did not do what was expected of him" and if he had the "spine to do it." The video then cuts back to black-and-white footage of an older man in a suit and striped tie, looking downward with a solemn expression, while a narrator's voice explains that "Sessions knew how things worked at the Justice Department" and had been a United States Attorney in Alabama, building a reputation as a "rock-ribbed conservative" which formed the foundation of his run for the Senate. This narration continues over a color interview with Jeffrey H. Smith, former CIA General Counsel, who is seen wearing glasses, a dark suit, and a maroon tie in a dimly lit setting, but does not speak in this segment. The narrative shifts to archival black-and-white footage of a man in a suit gesturing emphatically while speaking at a table, then to another man speaking into a microphone, as the narrator quotes a campaign message about undermining "honesty, hard work, and discipline." This leads into a vintage television screen displaying a 1996 campaign ad for Jeff Sessions for U.S. Senate, featuring him in a blue shirt with a woman and child in the background, accompanied by a voice-over declaring, "For Senate, Jeff Sessions." The final shot returns to black-and-white, showing a man in a light shirt and dark tie, seated and looking off to the side with a pensive expression, possibly in a courtroom or formal setting, as the narrator concludes that Sessions "served in the Senate for 20 years as a law and order conservative," followed by another male voice briefly stating, "I've been beat up by Jeff Sessions in..."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with Jeh Johnson, former Secretary of Homeland Security, dressed in a pinstripe suit and light blue tie, speaking seriously in a dimly lit interview setting. He discusses "Senate Judiciary hearings" and describes Jeff Sessions as "very predictable, hardline, conservative." The scene then transitions to a vibrant outdoor political rally in Madison, Alabama, where Donald Trump, wearing a red 'Make America Great Again' cap, a dark suit, and white shirt, walks through a dense, cheering crowd, waving and acknowledging supporters amidst loud applause and crowd noise. The crowd holds signs like 'TRUMP' and 'MAKE AMERICA GREAT AGAIN!', with many capturing the moment on their phones. A narrator's voice explains that "In 2016, Sessions decided to play a long shot," supporting Donald Trump with the belief he'd become Attorney General. Trump takes the stage at a podium with a blue 'TRUMP' sign and American flag bunting, where J.D. Gordon, a Trump campaign adviser, joins him. Gordon, with an energetic rally voice, exclaims, "And I want to just introduce you to him for a second. Senator Jeff Sessions!" to roaring applause. The video then cuts back to an interview with J.D. Gordon, who, in a dark suit with a purple tie and an American flag pin, states in a calm, articulate tone that Sessions "was invaluable to the campaign in helping to get President Trump elected." The rally footage returns, showing Trump and Gordon at the podium, with Gordon's energetic voice declaring, "Wow! What a crowd this is!" as the crowd continues to cheer. Gordon's interview voice further explains that Sessions "was the first senator to endorse President Trump" and held a rally with him in Alabama, noting that "at the time, people didn't know if he'd be the president or not." The segment concludes with Gordon's rally voice saying, "At this time in American's history, we..." as the audio fades.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with Donald Trump, wearing his signature red 'Make America Great Again' cap, dark suit, and white shirt, at a rally in Madison, Alabama. He gestures from a podium adorned with 'TRUMP' and patriotic bunting, his amplified voice declaring, "need to make America great again," to a cheering crowd holding supportive signs. The scene then shifts to Cameron Smith, identified as a former Jeff Sessions aide, speaking in a dimly lit interview setting. He calmly explains that "Because Sessions endorsed Trump, Trump didn't have to prove that he was a conservative, because Jeff Sessions is sort of the good housekeeping seal of approval for a lot of conservatives, particularly in the South where Trump was very successful." The video briefly returns to the rally where Trump, his amplified voice stating, "I'm proud to have you with us. God bless," walks off stage to applause. This is followed by a montage of newspaper headlines, such as 'Russian officials were in contact with Trump allies, diplomat says', while a male narrator states, "Trump appointed Sessions as Attorney General." The narrator continues, "But from almost the beginning, there was a problem," as the headlines transition to a chaotic hallway scene with reporters and cameras surrounding a man. A female reporter's voice adds, "As two more Trump campaign officials face scrutiny over their contacts with Russia." The narrative then moves to a formal, brightly lit hearing room, showing Senator Al Franken and Senator Jeff Sessions, who wears a red patterned tie and has a 'Sen. Jeff Sessions' nameplate. The narrator explains, "Interactions between Russians and the Trump campaign were becoming public. And at his confirmation hearing, Sessions was asked about them." In the same hearing room, Senator Al Franken's clear, formal voice is heard, quoting a CNN story: "CNN has just published a story, say quote, 'There was a continuing exchange of information during the campaign between Trump surrogates and intermediaries for the Russian government.'" Senator Franken's voice is heard again, beginning to address "Senator Franken..." before the audio cuts.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Senator Jeff Sessions, an older man with white hair in a dark suit and red patterned tie, seated at a formal hearing with his nameplate visible. He speaks into a microphone with a serious, attentive expression, stating in a clear, formal voice, "I'm not aware of any of those activities... I didn't have not have communications with the Russians." Other men in suits are seen behind him in the well-lit, official room. A mechanical whirring sound accompanies a close-up transition to a metallic, industrial-looking device with moving parts and a glowing light, suggesting archival or investigative work. A male narrator then explains, "But before long, The Washington Post reported Sessions had in fact met on at least two occasions with the Russian ambassador Sergey Kislyak." This is visually reinforced by a montage of 'The Washington Post' newspaper clippings from February 10, 2017, with headlines like 'Sessions’ Russia troubles' and red-underlined text detailing his undisclosed meetings. The narrator continues, "Senator Jeff Sessions spoke twice last year with Russia's ambassador to the United States, Justice Department officials said, encounters he did not disclose." The video cuts to Sessions outside, still in his dark suit and red tie, walking near a black SUV with a Virginia license plate, speaking to someone off-camera. The scene returns to newspaper clippings, showing headlines such as 'For Sessions, questions of perjury,' accompanied by a black-and-white photo of Sessions. His voice returns, defensively stating, "Well, I have not met with any Russians at any time to discuss any political campaign and those remarks are unbelievable to me and are false." A female reporter's voice is then heard, reporting, "Republicans urged the Trump White House to quickly resolve the controversy," followed by another male reporter stating, "Attorney General Jeff Sessions has repeatedly resisted," before the narrator concludes, "In the face of an onslaught of..." The overall tone is investigative and serious, highlighting the contradiction between Sessions’ testimony and the revealed contacts.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a montage of black-and-white Washington Post newspaper clippings from February 10, 2017, displaying headlines like 'Sessions’ Russia troubles' and 'For Sessions, questions of perjury,' with red-underlined text highlighting his undisclosed meetings. A deep, serious male voice states that the "story became impossible for Sessions to ignore." Philip Rucker, from The Washington Post, then appears in a dimly lit close-up, stating that "He was compromised. Jeff Sessions misled the Senate during his confirmation hearing about that. He had lied about whether he had that meeting." As a document titled 'Justice Dept. ethics guide' on 'Motions for Recusal or Disqualification' is shown, the narrator explains that "Department of Justice staff advised Sessions to recuse himself from the investigation." A woman with shoulder-length gray hair in a dark blazer speaks seriously in a dimly lit room, asserting with a clear, authoritative tone that "It was appropriate for him to recuse, and I think the appearance of impropriety, if not the reality, is something that's that was really important to avoid." The narrator then reveals, "But the President had disagreed. He wanted Sessions to be his first line of defense against Comey." Yamiche Alcindor from PBS NewsHour, in a green dress, speaks expressively, explaining that "The President expects of Jeff Sessions and of really a lot of people around him, complete loyalty," and that "He wants people to protect him. He wants people to, if possible, bend the rules a bit so that they can make sure that they have his back." The scene then shifts to a dramatic black-and-white close-up of Donald Trump, his face partially shadowed, conveying intensity, followed by a bright, sunny view of an aircraft carrier deck where a man in a dark suit and others walk toward a 'UNITED STATES OF AMERICA' helicopter. Over these final visuals, a male commentator remarks, "The Democrats, as you can imagine, having a field day with this," before another voice briefly mentions "lawmakers."

He wants people to, if possible, bend the rules a bit so that they can make sure that they have his back.'

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a dramatic tone as Donald Trump disembarks from a military helicopter, marked 'UNITED STATES OF AMERICA,' onto the sunlit deck of a huge new aircraft carrier in Newport News, Virginia, under a clear blue sky. He wears a red 'USA' cap, dark suit, and patterned tie, greeted by saluting naval officers and military personnel in dress uniforms. A male narrator explains that Trump's visit is a 'big event' and a 'photo op to send a message to Jeff Sessions.' Trump then walks alongside a group of men in suits and military officers, including one in a flight jacket with 'CVN 71' and a U.S. Navy patch, through the carrier’s interior corridors and into a dimly lit control room filled with monitors. Here, on-screen text appears, posing urgent questions from reporters: 'Mr. President, do you still have confidence in your campaign manager?' and 'Can Sessions recuse himself from investigations into your campaign?' A female reporter's voice is heard asking these very questions, along with follow-ups about Sessions' contact with the Russian ambassador. The narrator further explains that the President had already insisted his counsel tell Sessions not to recuse himself, and now Trump was saying it publicly. The scene abruptly shifts to Jeff Sessions, an older man with white hair, at a Department of Justice podium, with the DOJ seal and American flag behind him. The narrator reveals that without consulting Trump, Sessions called this press conference. Sessions, wearing a dark suit and red patterned tie and holding a green cup, formally addresses reporters, stating in his own clear voice, 'I have now decided to recuse myself from any existing or future investigations of any matter relating in any way to the campaigns for President of the United States,' before concluding with 'Thank you all, take care.' Throughout the segment, a consistent background music track underscores the unfolding political drama.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Senator Jeff Sessions, an older man with white hair in a dark suit and red patterned tie, walking away from a Department of Justice podium in Washington, D.C., against a backdrop of the DOJ seal and an American flag. Dramatic, investigative music plays throughout. The scene transitions to a dimly lit interview setting where Harvard Law School Professor Alan Dershowitz, wearing glasses, a dark suit, blue shirt, and striped tie, speaks directly to the camera about Trump losing his best chance to shut down an investigation and his frustration with an appointee who 'abdicated responsibility.' Following this, former Independent Counsel Robert Ray, also in a dark suit and tie, speaks in a similar interview setup, explaining that presidents feel a lack of power, though not typically so early in an administration. The video then cuts to a wide, overcast aerial shot of Washington D.C., showcasing the Lincoln Memorial, Washington Monument, and U.S. Capitol Building. As reporters' voices discuss FBI Director James Comey going public with testimony before Congress, and 'Sessions on the sidelines,' the final sequence shows Robert Mueller, a man in a dark suit and yellow-striped tie, walking through a government hallway flanked by police officers and aides. He then enters a formal, crowded hearing room, filled with reporters and officials, taking his seat at a large wooden table as cameras flash and onlookers watch intently, setting the stage for a high-stakes proceeding.

The video then cuts to a wide, overcast aerial shot of Washington D.C., showcasing the Lincoln Memorial, Washington Monument, and U.S. Capitol Building in the background. The final sequence shows a man in a dark suit and yellow-striped tie, identified as Robert Mueller, walking through a government hallway flanked by police officers and aides, then entering a formal hearing room filled with reporters and officials, where he takes his seat at a large wooden table as cameras flash and onlookers watch intently.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a chaotic scene inside a formal government hearing room, where a man in a dark suit and yellow-striped tie is being escorted away by uniformed officers, while reporters and photographers swarm the area, capturing the tense moment with flashing cameras. The scene then cuts to a calm interview setting with Jeh Johnson, a bald man in a pinstripe suit and light blue tie, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit room, reflecting on Jim Comey's remarkable communication and how Mr. Trump likely watched his testimony. The video returns to the hearing room, showing a wide shot of the crowded chamber with numerous reporters and officials, some holding cameras, and a digital timer displaying '4:55'. The focus shifts to a man in a navy uniform with gold epaulets, seated at the table, looking down at documents. The scene cuts again to a man visually identified as Robert Mueller, in a dark suit and yellow-striped tie, speaking at a podium, whose voice is heard formally testifying as Jim Comey, stating, "Mr. Chairman, ranking member shift members of the committee, thank you for including me in today's hearing. I'm honored to be here representing the people of the FBI. I have been authorized by the Department of Justice to confirm..." The video then transitions to an interview with Matt Apuzzo of The New York Times, a man with graying hair and a beard, wearing a dark blazer over a patterned shirt, speaking in a warmly lit room, analyzing how "all heads turned to the television" in anticipation of Comey's confirmation. The testimony (Comey's voice) continues, confirming, "That the FBI, as part of our counter-intelligence mission, is investigating the nature of any links between individuals associated with the Trump campaign and the Russian government, and whether there was any coordination between the campaign and Russia's..." Matt Apuzzo then comments on Comey confirming this to lawmakers. The video returns to the hearing room, showing a group of photographers and reporters capturing the event from the front row, with cameras pointed toward the podium. The final shot is a close-up of the man at the podium (visually Mueller, audibly Comey) speaking with a serious expression, as the digital timer now reads '4:53', emphasizing the gravity of the proceedings.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of Peter Baker from The New York Times, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit, warm-toned setting, wearing a dark suit, light blue shirt, and a patterned dark tie, with the FRONTLINE logo visible, as he states that something was perceived as "a direct and public threat to his presidency" in Trump's mind. Suspenseful, low-key orchestral background music plays throughout. The scene transitions to a brightly lit formal government hearing room with blue drapes, where multiple officials are seated at a long wooden table. The camera focuses on Mr. Nunes, identified by a nameplate as 'CHAIRMAN,' wearing a dark suit and red patterned tie, speaking with a serious expression, formally questioning, "do you have any evidence that any current Trump White House or administration official coordinated with the Russian intelligence services?" The scene cuts to James Comey, seated at the same hearing table, wearing a dark suit and yellow-striped tie, looking intently forward with a solemn demeanor, who concisely responds, "Not a question I can answer." The video then returns to a close-up of a man with glasses and graying hair, wearing a dark suit and blue polka-dot tie, speaking in a dimly lit room, analyzing that Comey's statement was "the death knell" and "the end of Jim Comey" in the president's mind. The hearing room is shown again, with Mr. Nunes speaking and gesturing slightly, remarking on a "big gray cloud" put over people. The camera returns to Comey, who maintains a composed, serious expression. The scene shifts to J.D. Gordon, identified by on-screen text as 'Dir., National Security, Trump campaign,' wearing a dark suit, purple tie, and an American flag pin, speaking in a formal interview setting, describing the situation as "surreal" and "a waking nightmare" that "put everyone close to President Trump under a cloud." The final shot is a black-and-white close-up of Donald Trump, wearing a dark suit and striped tie, standing in a doorway with a stern, pensive expression, his hand slightly raised, with curtains and a window visible behind him, as a male narrator's voice declares, "The head of the FBI dropped two bombshells landing at the White House doorstep," followed by a brief female voice saying, "Comey put..."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a black-and-white close-up of Donald Trump, wearing a dark suit and striped tie, standing in a doorway with a stern, pensive expression, his hand slightly raised. A male narrator's deep voice states, "With Flynn caught lying, and Sessions having recused himself, another showdown was becoming inevitable." The scene transitions to an exterior shot of the White House under overcast skies, with a wet pavement reflecting the building and surrounding trees, as the PBS logo appears. Another male narrator mentions, "A notorious Moscow bank is now part of the invest..." The camera then zooms in on the White House’s front facade, showing illuminated windows behind white columns and a black iron fence, with the FRONTLINE logo appearing. The first narrator continues, "And the press had also ratcheted up the pressure, as they uncovered more meetings between Russians and Trump campaign officials." The view shifts to a closer shot through the fence, emphasizing the building’s grandeur and security. The scene cuts to a man with graying hair and a beard, wearing a dark suit, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit, warm-toned setting, explaining, "Kushner had met with a top Russian banker who was known to be close to President Putin during the transition. He had also had a conversation with Sergey Kislyak, the Russian ambassador, and there had been even this discussion about setting up a backchannel communication to Moscow." This is followed by a black-and-white close-up of a man in profile, with a serious expression, partially obscured by shadows. The first narrator then identifies him, stating, "Many of the stories focused on Jared Kushner, the president's son-in-law." The video then cuts to a man with dark hair and glasses, wearing a dark suit, speaking with an animated expression in a dimly lit room, urgently remarking, "This is just not normal. It just seems like there's more and more meetings, just keep, they just keep coming out." The first narrator adds, "Now, Kushner was increasingly under scrutiny," as the final shot returns to the black-and-white profile of Jared Kushner, now shown in a more formal, centered composition, with a focused and intense gaze, reinforcing a sense of gravity and scrutiny. A female voice is heard saying, "Jared is learning that his actions, especially with Michael Flynn, his involvement..."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with Carol Leonnig from The Washington Post, speaking in a dimly lit interview setting, her voice discussing how "actions with various Russians is under investigation as well, and whether he's been honest about them and what was going on in those conversations." The scene transitions to black-and-white archival footage of Donald Trump and a younger man, likely Donald Trump Jr., walking through a formal room with American flags, as a male voice states, "Kushner headline today and tonight. The scandal had now reached the president's family." Carrie Johnson from NPR then appears in a similar interview setup, her voice explaining, "The notion that there's this investigation at the Justice Department and the FBI that could encompass his children, his son-in-law, and possibly his business interests, is getting him very, very nervous." A color clip follows, showing Donald Trump disembarking from Marine One under umbrellas in rainy weather, saluting military personnel, while the audio mentions "Jared Kushner, President's son-in-law" and describes Trump as "inundated by the headlines and under pressure from Comey," having "left Washington" for his country club in Bedminster, New Jersey. A female voice adds that it's a "rainy weekend in Bedminster," so Trump, "supposed to be out golfing," is "stuck inside" and "in a sort of foul mood anyway." Sarah Ellison from The Washington Post then speaks in a warm-lit interview, her voice stating, "The president was frustrated, his family was frustrated, they felt like they were being swept into this rip tide of an investigation." This is followed by a shot of a black SUV driving on a wet driveway toward a stately brick house. The final segment features Robert Costa, Moderator of Washington Week, speaking directly to the camera, his voice continuing the commentary about the investigation, noting "they thought it was..." (audio cuts off).

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a black-and-white close-up of Donald Trump, wearing a 'MAKE AMERICA GREAT AGAIN' cap and a 'TRUMP' jacket, speaking animatedly with expressive hand gestures against a dark background, as a male narrator discusses the possibility of ending an investigation by removing Comey. Tense background music underscores the narration, which then details "new whirlwind developments" in Bedminster on a rainy weekend, where Trump, without senior staff, made a consequential decision. The scene cuts to Sarah Ellison from The Washington Post, a woman with shoulder-length blonde hair in a purple blouse, speaking directly to the camera in a warmly lit setting, stating there was "essentially no adult in the room," specifically no "legal adult" to understand the political and legal implications. The video returns to black-and-white footage of Trump, first in a stern, pensive close-up, then in profile holding a phone to his ear, as the narrator explains Trump decided he "would get rid of Comey." Next, Carol Leonnig from The Washington Post, a woman with short brown hair and a red beaded necklace, speaks seriously in a dimly lit room, describing Trump's conclusion to fire Jim Comey based on "gut instinct and raw anger" without consultation. She later characterizes a dictated letter to Comey as a "rant," where Trump was "unloading all of the reasons that Comey has failed him." The final scene shifts to color, showing Donald Trump descending the red-carpeted stairs of Air Force One in a dark suit, with Melania Trump following behind in a light coat and dark skirt, as the presidential seal is visible on the aircraft door, while the narrator mentions "On Sunday, Donald Trump..."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens at night with Donald Trump, dressed in a dark suit, descending the red-carpeted stairs of Air Force One, followed closely by Melania Trump, as a narrator states he is "determined to carry out his plan to stop Jim Comey once and for all." The scene transitions to a daytime aerial view of the White House, framed by the Washington Monument, establishing a political context, followed by a close-up of a dimly lit interior through a window. A series of black-and-white, stylized shots of men in suits with serious or contemplative expressions appear, as a female voice comments that "Comey has been indicating that he knows so much more than he's letting on," and a male voice adds that "Comey opens up another investigation" into potential "collusion between Trump associates." The narrator then explains that "Trump was preparing to take the fateful step of sending the letter." A woman in a blue blazer, speaking directly to the camera with an earnest expression, reveals that "Word gets back to Don McGahn, the White House counsel, that this document has been prepared. And he freaks out," with another male voice quoting McGahn saying, "you don't want to send that." The narrator adds that "Even Trump's abrasive advisor Steve Bannon was stunned," and the woman in the blue blazer continues, quoting Bannon, "'You can't get rid of this guy, Jim Comey. This would be a terrible, terrible mistake. It's going to cause a firestorm.'" The final shot is a black-and-white close-up of a man, inferred to be Jim Comey, at a table surrounded by microphones and glasses, appearing under intense scrutiny with a stern and focused demeanor, as the narrator concludes that "Although Trump had the executive power to fire Comey..."

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a tense, investigative background score accompanying a series of stark black-and-white close-ups of political figures, starting with a stern, contemplative man in a dark suit and striped tie seated at a table. The scene then transitions to Jane Mayer, identified as 'The New Yorker,' speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit room, wearing a dark blazer, as she explains that 'Bannon has more of a sense of history than a lot of the people who are in that White House,' understanding 'the history of Watergate' and 'about obstruction of justice and what it could look like if Trump fires Comey.' The visuals cut back to black-and-white footage of two serious men in suits standing side by side, followed by a shadowed man in profile, a somber man in glasses, and a grave man with graying hair and a beard looking directly at the camera. A male voice, likely a narrator, dramatically quotes 'McGahn' asserting, 'There's no way... that this document can be used as the basis for firing Jim Comey. No way, no how. Give me the document.' The narrator continues, explaining that 'The White House Counsel had an alternative,' and 'McGahn had separately learned that Rod Rosenstein, the Deputy Attorney General, also had concerns with Jim Comey.' The narrator describes a deal being brokered, quoting a figure telling the President, 'You should really talk to Rod Rosenstein.' The video then shifts to color, showing a man in a suit and red tie walking briskly past a black SUV, as the narrator states, 'They set up a meeting between Rosenstein, Attorney General Jeff Sessions, and the President.' The final frames return to black-and-white, featuring a dramatically silhouetted man with glasses in profile and a pensive man with glasses looking downward, while the narrator concludes that 'The President lets them know he wants to fire James Comey, that's clear, and the directive for Sessions and Rosenstein is...' before the audio fades.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a man in a dark suit and patterned tie, speaking with a serious, authoritative male voice about someone being a 'professional' whose reputation was 'well earned,' all set against a dramatic, investigative musical score. The visual transitions to black-and-white footage of another man in a suit and glasses, conveying a somber tone, as a male narrator states that 'Rosenstein would build a case against Jim Comey's handling of the FBI.' The scene shifts to a woman in a blue blazer, speaking earnestly with expressive hand gestures, her clear, articulate female voice explaining that Rod Rosenstein, a 'boy scout' who served 27 years in the Justice Department, believed Comey 'violated the Justice Department norms by talking too much about Hillary Clinton during the election.' An exterior night shot of the grand J. Edgar Hoover FBI Building establishes the federal law enforcement context, while the narrator explains that 'The President wanted the memo as soon as possible. It was a rush job. He delivered it the next day.' The video then cuts to a close-up of a legal memorandum dated May 9, 2017, from Rod J. Rosenstein, Deputy Attorney General, with key phrases underlined in red, such as 'the Director made serious mistakes' and 'the conclusion of the email investigation was wrong.' The man in the dark suit and glasses returns, speaking directly to the camera, his serious voice echoing that 'Rod Rosenstein's memo echoed what a lot of the Hillary Clinton campaign people had been saying for months. That Comey had inserted himself into the election, he made himself too public, he had taken on a role that did not really belong to him.' The final shot focuses on another section of the memorandum, underlining 'the Director ignored another longstanding principle: we do not hold press conferences to release derogatory information about the subject of a criminal investigation,' as the male narrator emphasizes this breach of protocol, maintaining the overall investigative and serious tone.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a black-and-white archival-style shot of Donald Trump, seated at a desk resembling the Oval Office, wearing a dark suit and American flag pin, with a surreal animated horse floating above his head as he gestures. A male voice, later identified as Michael Isikoff, states that "Trump doesn't care about what Comey did to hurt Hillary Clinton, but it becomes the excuse... the White House uses to explain why they were firing the FBI director." The scene transitions to a color interview with Michael Isikoff, co-author of 'Russian Roulette,' who continues, explaining that "Donald Trump had fired hundreds of people face-to-face on The Apprentice. This time, as president, it would be different." An exterior shot of the White House under overcast skies, with a Secret Service car and barricades, then appears as another male voice, later identified as Timothy O’Brien, describes how Trump "sends his long-time bodyguard in a White House car with the pink slip over to the FBI to deliver the bad news." Timothy O’Brien, author of 'TrumpNation,' is then seen in a color interview, elaborating that "Keith Schiller, the president's body man, can't get into the FBI," and that the FBI is not a place where one can simply walk in with a note for James Comey from the White House. He concludes that the bodyguard "dropped off the letter and left." A black-and-white shot of Jeff Sessions walking with a stern expression is shown, followed by Matt Apuzzo of The New York Times in a color interview. The video then cuts to a close-up of the J. Edgar Hoover FBI Building sign, and a black-and-white shot of a man, possibly James Comey, standing in profile looking downward, as a female news reporter's voice announces, "Breaking news, James Comey has been removed from heading the FBI."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a dramatic black-and-white close-up of a man in a dark suit, his face partially shadowed, suggesting a tense moment, accompanied by tense, dramatic music. This transitions to a color interview with Bob Anderson, identified as 'Fmr. FBI Exec. Asst. Director,' who, with a composed and serious demeanor, recounts how then-FBI Director James Comey was giving a talk in Los Angeles when news broke that he had been fired by the President. The scene cuts back to the black-and-white profile shot of the man, now gesturing, before returning to a close-up of Bob Anderson, his expression more intense, as he describes Comey's initial disbelief, thinking it was a mistake, until a staff member confirmed the reality of the firing. News reports interject, stating, "President Trump has fired James Comey as Director of the FBI. It comes without warning," and calling it "a big shock" that "they did not see this coming at the FBI." An aerial view then shows a high-security motorcade of black SUVs and a limousine exiting a building, driving under an overpass, and along a road lined with palm trees, suggesting a high-profile departure in a warm climate, with news reports mentioning "News helicopters were waiting as Comey left the FBI field office." The video cuts to Frank Montoya, Jr., 'Fmr. FBI Special Agent in Charge,' who, with a focused and earnest expression, describes Comey's firing as "a major sucker punch," noting that Comey was "widely regarded throughout the organization," even by those who weren't "fans." The segment concludes with an aerial shot of a private jet on a tarmac, surrounded by black vehicles and personnel, hinting at a high-profile arrival or departure, while a news report draws comparisons to "President Richard Nixon's Saturday Night Massacre."

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a close-up of a man in a dark suit and light blue shirt, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit, professional setting, his voice discussing someone being "dismissed or fired." The scene quickly transitions to an aerial view of a white private jet, registration N834X, accelerating down a runway and lifting off into the sky, as a news anchor's voice announces "Moments ago, breaking news that no one saw coming today, we learned that President Trump has fired..." Another male voice adds that it was "a very closely kept secret here at the White House." The footage then shifts to a nighttime aerial shot of the U.S. Capitol Building, illuminated against the dark cityscape with the Washington Monument visible, while a voice mentions "studying unprecedented Comey." A dramatic voiceover declares "Shock, anger, and chaos engulfed Washington," as the camera pans to the White House, also lit at night. A female reporter's voice describes "mounting outrage on Capitol Hill," with some lawmakers "questioning the country's very foundation." Outside, a group of reporters and journalists are gathered, holding microphones and recording devices, as a voiceover explains that "At the White House, they struggled to offer an explanation," and another reporter states, "The White House is not interested in getting to the bottom of this." The audio continues, detailing how the "press office is suddenly thrust out there to explain a decision they had no part in," and that "it was chaos" for reporters trying to figure out what was going on. The final shot is a television screen showing Sean Spicer, identified as 'White House Press Secretary' on a Fox News broadcast, speaking with a serious expression, with the White House visible in the background on the screen. A female news anchor's voice confirms, "The Comey firing came without warning," and a male voiceover adds, "By the time the White House hit the airwaves, the story the press office told was that Rosenstein's memo was the primary reason for the firing." Spicer's voice is then heard saying, "Deputy Attorney General..."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a Fox News split-screen featuring Lou Dobbs and White House Press Secretary Sean Spicer. Spicer, in a dark suit and blue patterned tie, is heard in a clear, professional male voice explaining the firing of FBI Director Comey, referencing Rod Rosenstein's determination that the director had lost confidence. The scene then cuts to a FRONTLINE interview where Carrie Johnson of NPR, a woman with shoulder-length dark hair in a blue blazer, speaks directly to the camera in a dimly lit setting. Her clear, assertive female voice states the White House's message: "we fired Comey because he botched the Hillary Clinton investigation. Period." The video transitions to a CNN broadcast with a 'BREAKING NEWS' banner, showing a split-screen of Anderson Cooper, with white hair and glasses, and Kellyanne Conway, Counselor to the President. Cooper, in a dark suit, is heard in his distinct, calm, and inquisitive male voice asking, "to those who say why now, why fire James Comey now, what do you say?" Conway, wearing a light-colored blouse and gold necklace, responds with a composed, articulate female voice, pointing to "the three letters that were received today," including those from President Trump, Attorney General Sessions, and Deputy Attorney General Rod Rosenstein, to whom the FBI Director reports. A brief cut shows Howard Kurtz, author of 'Media Madness,' speaking in a dimly lit room. His clear, analytical male voice discusses how "all the people spinning on behalf of the White House told the press that the Comey firing was based on a memo from Rod Rosenstein... which had to do with Comey's performance in the Hillary Clinton investigation. Well, the press wasn't buying that." The CNN broadcast returns, with Cooper's voice interjecting, challenging that "most of this letter focuses on on Hillary Clinton's emails, this is stuff that as a can they Donald Trump praised James Comey for." The scene briefly returns to Kurtz, whose voice describes the situation as "a mind-bending situation, right? Because..." The news segments maintain consistent studio blue backdrops and professional attire, while FRONTLINE interviews feature softer, more intimate lighting.

The CNN segment maintains a serious tone, with Cooper occasionally nodding and Conway gesturing subtly as she speaks, emphasizing the gravity of the news. The lighting remains consistent across the news segments, with studio blue backdrops and professional attire, while the FRONTLINE interviews feature softer, more intimate lighting.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a man with graying hair and a beard, wearing a dark suit, speaking directly to the camera in a warm-toned room, commenting on the President's decision to fire the FBI director and questioning "what is going on here?" The scene transitions to a CNN broadcast split-screen featuring Anderson Cooper, a male anchor, on the left, and Kellyanne Conway, Counselor to the President, on the right, discussing the breaking news of 'TRUMP FIRES FBI DIRECTOR COMEY.' Conway explains that the President "took the recommendation of his Deputy Attorney General," to which Cooper interjects, "That makes no sense." Conway defends the decision, stating it "makes complete sense because he has lost confidence in the FBI director." Cooper then identifies the Deputy Attorney General as "Rod Rosenstein." The broadcast cuts to an exterior night shot of the U.S. Department of Justice building, with illuminated signage. A man with glasses, dark hair, and a dark suit then appears, speaking seriously in a dimly lit room, stating that "Rosenstein was surprised he was receiving all the blame." Conway's voice is heard again, reiterating that "President Trump fired Comey because of Rosenstein's recommendation." The man with glasses continues, explaining that "Rod Rosenstein sees what's happening and that his reasons are being used as the pretext to justify the President's action," adding that Rosenstein "was none too pleased." Conway's voice is heard once more, noting that "Rod Rosenstein is not particularly happy that the White House is painting the blame on him," before the man with glasses concludes, "Rosenstein is blown..." in a dramatic black-and-white profile shot, his face partially shadowed.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a dramatic black-and-white close-up of a silhouetted man, likely James Comey, in deep contemplation, as a female voice (Carol Leonnig) discusses someone threatening to resign if the president continues a certain course of action. The scene cuts to Carol Leonnig, speaking with a serious demeanor, her commentary underscored by tense, dramatic music. A shift to color reveals the White House, followed by a Fox News broadcast with a chyron announcing 'COMEY OUT: PRESIDENT ABRUPTLY FIRES FBI DIRECTOR.' News anchors, both male and female, discuss Rod Rosenstein's memo and question if Comey was fired due to White House fears. A male narrator then identifies Fox News as 'the president's go-to network,' as a dramatic female anchor declares, 'You're fired! President Trump ousting the FBI Director James Comey.' The narrator continues, stating the news was 'all Comey, all the time,' while another male anchor mentions a live report from Kevin Corke. The visuals then show a Marine opening a car door and a white vehicle driving away, as the narrator reveals the president celebrated Comey's firing behind closed doors with 'unlikely White House guests': Russian Foreign Minister Sergey Lavrov and Ambassador Sergey Kislyak. Carol Leonnig returns, sighing 'Oy' and commenting on 'That meeting,' which a female anchor describes as with 'the highest-ranking Russian official the president has met so far,' before Leonnig trails off, describing it as 'like a...'
segments.visual_original_caption: The video opens with a dramatic black-and-white close-up of a man in profile, wearing glasses and a suit, his face partially shadowed by high-contrast lighting, suggesting introspection or tension. The scene cuts to Carol Leonnig of The Washington Post, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit interview setting, wearing a black sleeveless top and a bold red beaded necklace. The video then returns to the silhouetted man, now bowed in contemplation, reinforcing a mood of gravity. A shift to color reveals the White House viewed through a black iron fence, establishing a political context. This is followed by a rapid transition to a Fox News broadcast, with a chyron reading 'COMEY OUT: PRESIDENT ABRUPTLY FIRES FBI DIRECTOR,' showing a man in a suit raising his hand under oath, likely James Comey, and then a news panel with three anchors discussing the event. The scene cuts to a Marine in dress uniform opening a car door, followed by a brief shot of a white vehicle driving away. The video returns to Carol Leonnig, who continues her commentary with expressive gestures and a serious demeanor, emphasizing the significance of the events being discussed. The overall narrative flows from personal introspection to public political upheaval, using stark visuals and news footage to convey urgency and consequence.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a black-and-white scene of Donald Trump, Sergei Lavrov, and another man in suits, where Trump is seen laughing heartily and placing a hand on Lavrov’s shoulder, creating a jovial atmosphere. A female voice, later identified as Carrie Johnson, comments that 'you can't believe it really happened. But the president is essentially celebrating with the Russian diplomat.' This is followed by a cut to Carrie Johnson from NPR, speaking with a serious expression, as an unidentified male voice discusses the firing of the man heading the probe into Trump campaign ties to Russia. The video returns to Trump laughing with the other two men, while Carrie Johnson's voice highlights that 'No US-based reporters. No American White House reporters are in the room.' A man with a beard and gray hair, wearing a dark suit, then appears in a dimly lit interview setting, as a brief Russian voice is heard, followed by an English male voice explaining that 'The Russians came in with a photographer from their state media agency TASS who took photos of this event,' which were 'used to some effect in Russia as propaganda.' The black-and-white scene of Trump gesturing and laughing reappears, further emphasizing the informal atmosphere. Karen DeYoung from The Washington Post is then shown speaking directly to the camera, while an unidentified male voice remarks on the 'terrible optics' of the situation, stating, 'you couldn't have scripted it worse.' The scene briefly returns to Trump, as a male voice quotes him saying, 'we're gonna have a great relationship,' and describing the investigation as a 'total irritant for me,' adding that 'Jim Comey's firing lifted a great weight for me,' and calling Comey 'a nut job.' The segment concludes with a montage of black-and-white Washington Post headlines, including 'COMFY FIRESTORM' and 'TRUMP LASHES OUT AT COMEY AND CRITICS,' with red underlines, as a male voice declares, 'First the firing, now the fallout,' and notes that 'the negative reaction to the Comey firing was gaining momentum.' Throughout, a consistent, slightly tense background music underscores the unfolding political controversy.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a montage of sensational newspaper headlines like 'TRUMPER TANTRUM' and 'FED UP,' highlighting media reactions to Donald Trump's firing of James Comey, accompanied by a dramatic news-style intro sound. A female voice states, "President Trump now facing outrage after firing Comey," followed by a male voice noting, "It is hard to over... To address the crisis, the president went on his old network for a one-on-one interview." The scene transitions to a television screen displaying an 'NBC NIGHTLY NEWS EXCLUSIVE TRUMP INTERVIEW,' with a voiceover announcing, "This is NBC Nightly News with Lester Holt. Tonight, stunning revelations from President Trump in our NBC News exclusive interview." Trump, initially seen in a dark suit with a red tie against a White House backdrop, is then shown walking through a grand, dimly lit room with red carpeting, flanked by Secret Service and media. He sits down for an interview with NBC News anchor Lester Holt, who wears a dark suit and blue polka-dot tie, holding a notepad. In this formal room, adorned with American flags and the Presidential Seal, Trump, now in a blue striped tie and an American flag pin, speaks animatedly, gesturing with his hands. Holt intently asks, "Monday you met with the Deputy Attorney General Rod Rosenstein... Did you ask for a recommendation?" Trump responds, "What I did is I was going to fire Comey. My decision. It was not..." Holt interjects, "You had made the decision before they came in the room," to which Trump firmly states, "I I was going to fire Comey." This interview is interspersed with commentary from Matthew Miller, a former Department of Justice official (2009–11), who appears in a dimly lit setting, wearing a dark suit and purple tie, speaking directly to the camera. Miller remarks, "It is a dramatic moment to see the president come out and not only completely undermine the case that his White House had been making... The president comes out and demolishes that case immediately." Trump is then heard again, asserting, "I was going to fire regardless of recommendation," as Miller continues, "I think there's a level on which President Trump doesn't want to be portrayed as just doing the bidding of some aides who write a memo." The segment concludes with a brief return to Trump mid-speech, emphasizing his expressive demeanor and the serious tone of the interview.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of Donald Trump, formally dressed in a dark suit, blue striped tie, and American flag pin, speaking expressively with a serious demeanor against a backdrop of the American flag and Presidential Seal. He is heard stating, "He's the decider... this Russia thing with Trump and Russia is a made-up story, it's an excuse by the Democrats for having lost an election that they should have won." A dramatic, investigative background score begins and continues throughout. The scene transitions to Philip Rucker of The Washington Post, identified by on-screen text, who, with a beard and dark hair, wearing a dark suit and purple tie, speaks directly to the camera in a dimly lit setting, commenting on Trump: "he often says what he believes, and if you just wait long enough, he'll tell you the truth." The video then cuts to a nighttime exterior shot of the illuminated White House, followed by a dramatic black-and-white close-up of Trump with his eyes closed, and then a profile shot of him smiling in a white cap behind a fence. During these visuals, a narrator's voice explains that "The interview backfired. The President's comments contradicted the White House. It triggered questions about whether Comey's firing was an attempt to obstruct justice by the President," and highlights "contradictions and confusion from the White House over the President admitting Russia was on his mind." The segment concludes with a woman in a blue blazer and black top, with shoulder-length blonde hair, speaking animatedly with hand gestures, stating that "For his part, even out of a job, Jim Comey was not going to be sidelined. Jim Comey, who's not known for staying silent... begins to defend him."

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a black-and-white, grainy shot of a man in a suit entering a dimly lit garage, setting a tense, clandestine atmosphere. A narrator explains that Comey decided to force the Justice Department to name a special counsel, as the scene transitions to a close-up of a typed memo with the phrase 'He is a good guy' underlined in red, highlighting a pivotal moment. A montage of newspaper printing presses in motion symbolizes the dissemination of information, culminating in a close-up of a New York Times front page headline: 'TRUMP APPEALED TO COMEY TO HALT INQUIRY INTO AIDE,' with the subheading 'Ex-F.B.I. Chief Noted Request in Memo: ‘I Hope You Can Let This Go.’' A news reporter's voice details the memo's contents, stating it was written after an Oval Office meeting. Frank Montoya, Jr., a former FBI Special Agent in Charge, appears in a color interview, earnestly describing the event as 'Jim's leak,' adding that 'it was information that he had that he passed on to the New York Times,' and concluding, 'I think that was the straw that broke the camel's back.' A black-and-white shot of James Comey looking upward with a serious expression follows, as background voices discuss 'another cloud of controversy' and 'calls demanding a special prosecutor.' A woman in a blue blazer then speaks with intensity in a color interview, asserting that Jim Comey 'knows way more about political dynamics and the way Washington works than most people in this story.' The narrator confirms that 'as Comey hoped, the call for a special counsel grew louder,' before the segment concludes with an exterior view of the J. Edgar Hoover FBI Building at dusk, illuminated with American flags, establishing the institutional setting.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a man in a dark suit and purple tie, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit, formal setting. The scene transitions to a black-and-white shot of a man in a suit and striped tie, standing solemnly with hands clasped, evoking a serious mood. His voice begins, discussing how "Rod Rosenstein watched his reputation get dragged through the mud" and found that appointing a special counsel was the only way to "undo the damage." This is followed by a profile close-up of the same man, now wearing glasses, speaking with a focused expression as he quotes Rosenstein needing to "stabilize the investigation" and the "Department of Justice" which had been "under siege." A solemn voiceover then describes a special counsel as "the most powerful investigative weapon the Justice Department wields." The video then cuts to Robert Costa, identified by on-screen text as 'Moderator, Washington Week' under the 'FRONTLINE' logo, wearing a dark suit and red tie, speaking with a composed demeanor in a softly lit room. The sequence shifts to a dramatic black-and-white close-up of an older man with graying hair, wearing a suit and patterned tie, his face illuminated against a dark background, as the voiceover continues, stating that Rosenstein "named one of the nation's legendary prosecutors, former FBI Director Robert Mueller, to be the special counsel." The man with glasses then returns, emphasizing that in Mueller, "you have the ultimate presence who's discreet, but also experienced." The final shot features another man in a dark suit and light blue shirt, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit setting, his expression earnest and engaged, as he asserts that Mueller is "the guy who has no problem with holding people accountable, being direct and driven to get the answer," and that "he's going to do it right... in accordance with the rules." Throughout, the lighting alternates between warm, focused tones and stark black-and-white, emphasizing the weight of the narrative, with faint background audio of a female news anchor reporting "breaking news" about the White House in crisis and the Justice Department appointing a special counsel.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a man in a dark suit and light blue shirt, speaking seriously, asking if one should be worried about Bob Mueller and emphatically stating, "You should be afraid, you should be very afraid," all set to tense, dramatic background music. A dramatic black-and-white close-up of a man with graying hair, his face illuminated with an intense, contemplative expression, follows. Yamiche Alcindor, identified as from PBS NewsHour, is heard discussing the department naming a special counsel, while Chris Whipple, identified as an author, mentions the "Russia collusion probe," which J.D. Gordon, a Trump campaign adviser, adds "could expand." The scene shifts to a nighttime exterior of the White House, with a police officer in a yellow jacket and flashing police car lights, as the man in the dark suit narrates that "At the White House, the President happened to be meeting with Attorney General Sessions" when "Rosenstein called to announce Mueller's appointment." A White House window with a white object and an American flag is briefly shown. J.D. Gordon is then seen speaking in a formal interview, while Chris Whipple comments that "President Trump doesn't like to get bad news, and this was bad news. It was more than bad news, it was terrible news." Another black-and-white close-up of the man with graying hair, looking upward seriously, precedes Yamiche Alcindor, who speaks expressively, stating that Trump "unleash[ed] all his anger on Jeff Sessions, and plainly tells Jeff Sessions that you are the reason why all of this is happening." A black-and-white shot of Donald Trump speaking with an intense expression is shown. J.D. Gordon then adds that "Trump was furious and took it out on Sessions and humiliated him," and "obviously felt himself endangered by a special counsel and uh lost his temper." The segment concludes with a black-and-white close-up of Donald Trump in profile, looking downward solemnly, as Yamiche Alcindor states, "Trump's law is loyalty to him and what he wants," with the dramatic music persisting.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with NPR's Carrie Johnson, in a blue blazer over a black top, speaking expressively in a dimly lit interview setting, her voice heard asking, 'where is my Roy Cohn?' and discussing how Jeff Sessions' actions will not be forgiven by Donald Trump, as dramatic, tense background music begins. The scene transitions to a black-and-white close-up of a man’s face, likely James Comey, gazing upward contemplatively, while a male commentator states that Sessions 'had had enough of the president's anger.' Peter Baker from The New York Times, in a dark suit and patterned tie, then speaks directly to the camera in a warm-toned setting, as another male voice describes Sessions 'bolting out of the White House, rushing out to his car.' An outdoor scene follows, showing a man in a dark suit walking toward a black SUV with Virginia license plate 'WRP-2855', accompanied by another man, while a third male voice explains Sessions is 'resigning as Attorney General,' feeling 'distraught' and 'at the end of his rope' after being 'insulted by Trump.' The video cuts to a man in a blue suit and striped tie speaking seriously, as a fourth male voice declares, 'In the West Wing, all hell broke loose.' A nighttime shot of a white building's window, possibly the White House, with a dimly lit interior, appears next, while the third male voice recounts Don McGahn bursting into Priebus's office to announce a special counsel appointment and Sessions' resignation. A black-and-white close-up of a man in a suit, his face shadowed, is shown as a fifth male voice quotes Priebus saying, 'you're kidding me.' The man in the blue suit is seen speaking again, while the third male voice describes Priebus running to the West Wing parking lot, finding Sessions in his car, and banging on the door. The sequence continues with a black-and-white close-up of another man’s face with wide, intense eyes, followed by a man in a dark suit walking down a hallway with framed pictures, his expression stern. The segment concludes with Peter Baker, now gesturing with his hand, emphasizing a point in his commentary, all set against the persistent dramatic music.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of a man with dark hair and glasses, wearing a dark suit and patterned tie, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit interview setting, urgently stating, "You gotta come out, you gotta come back in, you can't leave this way. You can't just blow up like this." Dramatic, investigative music begins as the scene transitions to black-and-white footage of an older man in a suit and striped tie, looking out from a car window with a serious expression, while a male voice narrates about Priebus almost having to drag someone back into the West Wing, where Vice President Pence and Steve Bannon then join to "talk Sessions off the ledge." Mueller the III was just getting started" across town in a secure location. This is followed by a dramatic black-and-white close-up of an older man with graying hair, his face illuminated against a dark background, conveying gravity, while a male voice explains the demanding nature of becoming a special prosecutor, emphasizing the initial lack of staff, agents, prosecutors, or even basic supplies like a legal pad. The camera then pans across the modern glass facade of the J. Edgar Hoover FBI Building, before returning to black-and-white footage of a man in a suit and patterned tie, bowed and looking down, evoking distress. Finally, Robert Ray, identified as 'Fmr. Independent Counsel,' appears in a formal interview setting with a serious expression, wearing a dark suit, white shirt, and blue polka-dot tie, with the 'FRONTLINE' logo visible, briefly heard saying "reasonable suspicion." segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a black-and-white portrait of a young Robert Mueller in a Marine Corps dress uniform, his serious expression highlighted by a spotlight, as a somber, investigative score plays. A woman in a blue blazer, speaking with concern in a dimly lit room, explains that Mueller "volunteered to serve in Vietnam as a United States Marine, highly decorated, wounded in action." The scene transitions to black-and-white close-ups of an older Robert Mueller in a suit and tie, his contemplative expressions shown from various angles, including one seated before a bookshelf. A voiceover details that in the 90s, Mueller "had tried his hand in the private sector at a prestigious law firm. He hated it." Marc Fisher of The Washington Post, in a color interview, elaborates that despite earning "$400,000 a year, he felt like he wasn't doing the Lord's work. He quit." Michael Isikoff, Co-Author of 'Russian Roulette,' then appears in a color interview, speaking expressively about Mueller taking "a substantial pay cut to become a line prosecutor," working "homicide in Washington D.C.," and how his "great joy was putting away bad guys and answering his phone, Mueller, Homicide." The video returns to a black-and-white shot of Mueller standing before the FBI seal, featuring 'FIDELITY,' 'BRAVERY,' and 'INTEGRITY,' followed by a dramatic, high-contrast profile close-up emphasizing his stern expression. Marc Fisher's voice returns, stating, "Bob Mueller cares about one thing and one thing only... indicting bad guys and putting them in prison." A voiceover concludes that Mueller, "a Republican, he'd run the FBI for both George W. Bush and Barack Obama."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a dramatic black-and-white close-up of a man’s face, his expression intense and contemplative, as the audio introduces Robert Mueller being "back at the center of the action," having "quietly gathered a team of more than three dozen attorneys, investigators." The scene transitions to a modern glass building, likely the J. Edgar Hoover FBI Building, with reflections of an American flag and trees, establishing an official setting. A male voiceover explains that from a secure location, Mueller "built a formidable team," which one commentator describes as "ninja assassins of attorneys." The camera then cuts to a color shot of a younger man with dark hair and glasses, wearing a suit and red tie, speaking directly to the camera in a formal interview setting. He elaborates on Mueller's team, likening it to "the Avengers movies where all the superheroes are spread across the globe," called by "Bob Mueller" to "reassemble together in Washington to take on this new mission." This is followed by a series of black-and-white shots, including an older man with graying hair and a patterned tie walking with a serious expression, and a bald man with glasses adjusting his tie as he walks outdoors near a stately building. The commentator continues, stating that "Team Mueller has assembled, maybe the A-team of prosecutors for an entire generation," and begins naming key members: "Aaron Zebley, who is an FBI agent before becoming a prosecutor," and "Michael Dreeben, who is one of the smartest people I know," having "argued over 100 Supreme Court cases." The video then shows a woman with shoulder-length gray hair, wearing a dark top, speaking animatedly with her hands clasped, identified as "Jeannine Re, who is a highly respected prosecutor in the US Attorney's office." This is followed by a man with dark hair and glasses, speaking with a concerned expression, identified as "Andrew Weisman," who "has a reputation for being a scorch earth prosecutor." The audio also mentions "Mueller put Greg Andres on his team, who was an experienced mob prosecutor in New York." The final shot is a color close-up of an older man with gray hair and glasses, wearing a blue shirt and red patterned tie, speaking with expressive gestures in a warmly lit interview setting, concluding that this was "the first sort of warning sign for the Trump White House." The overall narrative flows from intense personal moments to formal institutional settings, blending black-and-white archival-style footage with color interviews to convey a story of political or legal significance, centered around the formation and impact of Mueller's team.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a dramatic black-and-white close-up of a man, his finger pressed to his lips, followed by a side profile of another man, both conveying intense seriousness, as a narrator's voice describes "killers, Steve Bannon calls them." The narrator continues, explaining that "Mueller's team had broad authority to investigate Russian interference, the Trump campaign," and in the wake of the Comey firing, "possible obstruction of justice by the President himself." The scene transitions to color, showing a man with glasses in a dark suit speaking directly to the camera, his voice explaining, "this is question of whether that act by itself constitutes obstruction of justice. Maybe it does, maybe it doesn't, but that issue didn't even exist before he fired James Comey, now it does. Now it's the subject of a federal investigation by a special prosecutor." The visual then cuts to a legal document, a May 9, 2017 memorandum from Rod J. Rosenstein, Deputy Attorney General, with key phrases like 'the Director made serious mistakes' and 'we do not hold press conferences to release derogatory information' underlined in red. The narrator's voice details how "Mueller also had the evidence from Comey's memos," specifically mentioning "The President asking Comey for loyalty. To go easy on Mike Flynn. The berating of Sessions for his recusal. The use of the Rosenstein memo." Further underlined text on the document highlights controversial statements such as 'he needed loyalty' and 'I hope you can let this go.' The segment concludes with Robert Bennett, identified as a former President Clinton lawyer, speaking in a dimly lit interview setting with the 'FRONTLINE' logo visible, his serious voice stating, "So, you know, you start stringing all these together, and that's how a prosecutor would present the case. He's improper acts, even if they are not in and of themselves criminal, amount..." A consistent, slightly dramatic background score plays throughout the segment.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a close-up of James Clapper, Fmr. Dir. of Natl. Intelligence, seated in a formal interview, wearing a dark suit, gray shirt, and purple polka-dot tie, glasses, and a serious expression, as he audibly states, "to her an intent to obstruct justice." Dramatic, tense background music begins, accompanying a transition to a black-and-white silhouette of a man, likely James Comey, in a suit, first in profile and then bowed, evoking contemplation. A male narrator's voice describes this period, stating, "The White House was under siege. The President in anger and desperation, returned to Roy Cohn's strategy, a forceful counter-attack." Overlaid on this image are animated Twitter posts from Donald J. Trump, with his distinct voice audibly proclaiming, "This is the single greatest witch hunt of a politician in American history," followed by, "There is no collusion and no obstruction. I should be given apology," and then, "You are witnessing the single greatest witch hunt in American political history, led by some very bad and conflicted people." These tweets are displayed with engagement metrics and the Twitter interface, emphasizing their public and viral nature. The scene then cuts to Lisa Desjardins of PBS NewsHour, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit setting with an earnest expression, explaining how "The President definitely seized on that term witch hunt... It's something that he felt was working to undermine the Mueller investigation." Another tweet from Trump appears, again with his voice stating, "After seven months of investigations and collusion with the Russians, nobody has been able to show any proof. Sad." The final shot is of Yamiche Alcindor of PBS NewsHour, wearing a green V-neck dress, speaking expressively with gesturing hands in a softly lit room, commenting that President Trump calling the Mueller investigation a "witch hunt has an impact in Washington." The overall tone is investigative and critical, focusing on Trump’s public denials and the political controversy surrounding the Russia investigation, all underscored by the persistent dramatic music.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a rapid montage of Fox News broadcasts, accompanied by an upbeat, dramatic news jingle, featuring a female anchor in a green top with a chyron 'GOP SLAMMED FOR NOT 'PROTECTING' PRESIDENT.' This quickly transitions to a shot of the Statue of Liberty with a news ticker about a California wildfire, then to the 'FOX & friends' set where three anchors are seated, discussing the 'WHITE HOUSE VS SPECIAL COUNSEL' as a male voice states, "And at Fox News, that's just what happened," followed by another male voice declaring, "The President is really mad," and a third adding, "He tweeted this out as the phony Russian witch hunt continues." A close-up of a male anchor appears with the chyron 'REPORTS: TRUMP LEGAL TEAM LOOKING INTO POSSIBLE CONFLICTS AMONG MUELLER'S HIRES,' followed by another male anchor discussing 'NEW DEVELOPMENTS IN DEMOCRATIC SCANDALS.' A split-screen shows two men discussing 'FBI CHIEF MCCABE FACES THREE SEPARATE FEDERAL INQUIRIES,' while a male voice, likely Howard Kurtz, emphatically states, "This is a very dangerous witch hunt," echoed by other male voices agreeing, "Only because I think this is a witch hunt," and calling to "put an end to the political witch hunt against President Trump." Howard Kurtz, identified as 'FOX News' under the 'FRONTLINE' logo, is seen speaking, as a male voice, likely Gabriel Sherman, explains that "Trump was avidly watching. He likes what he sees on these Fox opinion shows, and they often get the benefit of having access to the President." The montage continues with a male anchor presenting the chyron 'ANTI-TRUMP BIAS EXPOSED IN MUELLER PROBE,' followed by a split-screen of a female and male anchor with a graphic highlighting that 'more than half of the attorneys who have served on Mueller's team have contributed to Democrats.' During this, a male voice, likely Lou Dobbs, is heard exclaiming, "Lou, get rid of Mueller!" with other male voices echoing calls like, "Mueller should be dismissed," and "Robert Mueller must be fired immediately," and "The call for the firing of Robert Mueller." Lou Dobbs is then shown in a studio with the chyron 'CRITICIZES SESSIONS OVER RUSSIA RECUSAL,' as a male voice warns, "So he either pulls the plug now or this will be going on years from now." This is followed by a close-up of a male anchor reporting on 'KAREN HANDEL (R) WINS GEORGIA 6TH DISTRICT CONGRESSIONAL SPECIAL ELECTION,' while Gabriel Sherman's voice describes Trump's "daily conversations with the hosts there," enabling him to "get his talking points out there." Gabriel Sherman, identified as 'Author, The Loudest Voice in the Room' under 'FRONTLINE,' is seen speaking, as a male voice declares, "The FBI is a shadow government now." Another male voice questions, "And what they did in leaking this information was illegal, correct?" to which another responds, "Absolutely illegal and almost becomes a soft coup in a sense," with a final voice adding, "They're leaking..." The segment concludes with a male anchor presenting the chyron 'GOP LAWMAKERS CITE NEW ALLEGATIONS OF POLITICAL BIAS IN FBI,' a split-screen of two male anchors under the 'Hannity' logo, and finally, a female anchor in a red dress speaking against an American flag backdrop, all maintaining the intense, critical tone of the preceding discussions.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video segment opens with a montage of Fox News broadcasts, rapidly cutting between various anchors and programs. A female anchor in a green top appears on screen with a chyron reading 'GOP SLAMMED FOR NOT 'PROTECTING' PRESIDENT,' followed by a shot of the Statue of Liberty with a news ticker about a California wildfire. The sequence transitions to the 'FOX & friends' set, where three anchors—two men and one woman—are seated at a desk, with the chyron 'WHITE HOUSE VS SPECIAL COUNSEL' displayed. The broadcast then shifts to a close-up of a male anchor in a suit and tie, with a chyron stating 'REPORTS: TRUMP LEGAL TEAM LOOKING INTO POSSIBLE CONFLICTS AMONG MUELLER'S HIRES.' The scene cuts to another male anchor in a suit, with a chyron 'NEW DEVELOPMENTS IN DEMOCRATIC SCANDALS,' followed by a split-screen of two men in suits discussing 'FBI CHIEF MCCABE FACES THREE SEPARATE FEDERAL INQUIRIES.' The video then features Howard Kurtz, identified as 'FOX News' under the 'FRONTLINE' logo, speaking in a dimly lit interview setting. The montage continues with a male anchor in a suit and tie, with a chyron 'ANTI-TRUMP BIAS EXPOSED IN MUELLER PROBE,' followed by a split-screen of a female anchor and a male anchor with a graphic stating 'In fact, more than half of the attorneys who have served on Mueller's team have contributed to Democrats.' The segment then shows Lou Dobbs in a studio, with a chyron 'CRITICIZES SESSIONS OVER RUSSIA RECUSAL,' followed by a close-up of a male anchor with a chyron 'KAREN HANDEL (R) WINS GEORGIA 6TH DISTRICT CONGRESSIONAL SPECIAL ELECTION.' The video cuts to Gabriel Sherman, identified as 'Author, The Loudest Voice in the Room' under the 'FRONTLINE' logo, speaking in an interview. The final frames show a male anchor in a suit with a chyron 'GOP LAWMAKERS CITE NEW ALLEGATIONS OF POLITICAL BIAS IN FBI,' followed by a split-screen of two male anchors with the 'Hannity' logo, and concludes with a female anchor in a red dress speaking against a backdrop of an American flag.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a dynamic street-level shot of a yellow NYC taxi driving past the modern glass facade of The New York Times building, its iconic logo prominently displayed, as a female voice is heard discussing a 'cleansing needed in our FBI and Department of Justice.' The camera then tilts upward, emphasizing the building’s height, while a male narrator explains that 'At The New York Times that summer,' they had a lead on what would become 'the biggest story yet,' having discovered 'another meeting between the Trump campaign and the Russians.' This transitions to a close-up interview with Matt Apuzzo of The New York Times, identified by on-screen text under the FRONTLINE logo, who explains that he and his colleagues had been reporting on 'another Russian meeting that we didn't totally understand' and that 'had been undisclosed during the campaign.' The scene then shifts to a series of black-and-white close-ups of various individuals—a man in a suit and tie, a woman in a dark blazer with a necklace, and another man in profile, all with serious expressions—as the narrator details that 'Donald Trump Jr. had hosted the meeting' with Russian lawyer Natalia Veselnitskaya, also noting the presence of Jared Kushner and Paul Manafort. The video then moves to a formal diplomatic setting at the G20 Germany 2017 summit, where Donald Trump and Vladimir Putin are seated in white chairs, flanked by American and Russian flags, with photographers capturing the moment amidst the sound of camera clicks and murmuring. The camera zooms in on Putin as the narrator states that 'The newspaper wanted a comment from the White House,' but that week, the President was in 'Hamburg, Germany for the G20 summit and his first meeting with Vladimir Putin.' The segment concludes with an interview with Philip Rucker of The Washington Post, identified by on-screen text under the FRONTLINE logo, speaking in a dimly lit setting, describing the situation as 'a big distraction on the sidelines of the summit.' Throughout, a serious, investigative-style background music underscores the narrative.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens at the G20 Germany 2017 summit, where Donald Trump and Vladimir Putin are seen seated in white chairs, flanked by their national flags, engaging in conversation amidst aides and photographers. A man with graying hair and a beard, speaking in a dimly lit interview setting, explains the challenge of responding to a New York Times inquiry, quoting the White House's request for time. He then recounts how, after the summit, the president took control of the situation, and he received a call from an Air Force One operator asking for more time as they worked on a statement. As Air Force One is dynamically shown accelerating on a runway and lifting off into a clear sky, its 'UNITED STATES OF AMERICA' livery visible, the man reveals that Hope Hicks and President Trump were working on this statement at the front of the plane. A black-and-white close-up of Donald Trump in profile, speaking intensely to a woman, underscores his central role. Yamiche Alcindor, identified by on-screen text, then states that the president was physically dictating a message for his son, Donald Trump Jr. This is followed by a black-and-white close-up of a serious Donald Trump on a phone call inside an aircraft, an American flag pin on his lapel, while Carol Leonnig, also identified by on-screen text, comments that the president's lawyers were 'losing their minds' and clarifies they were not on Air Force One or in Germany.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a black-and-white, cinematic shot of Donald Trump seated at a large, dimly lit desk, speaking on a telephone with a serious expression, as a calm male voice discusses hearing secondhand about a statement being issued to the New York Times. This transitions to a color interview with Robert Bennett, identified as 'Fmr. Pres. Clinton lawyer,' who, in a dimly lit room, earnestly states in an exasperated tone that writing a statement is 'amateur hour' and that lawyers 'couldn't control their client' and 'still can't control their client.' The scene cuts back to a dramatic black-and-white close-up of Trump, his face illuminated from the side, as a news anchor's voice reports the White House's response to a New York Times report claiming Donald Trump Jr. met with someone. A deeper, authoritative voice then details Trump's statement, written for his son, about a meeting concerning the adoption of Russian orphans, followed by another male voice (possibly Trump Jr.) confirming it was a 'short introductory meeting' to discuss a program about Russian children. A montage of black-and-white newspaper clippings, with red-highlighted text, visually reinforces the narrative of political scrutiny, while the authoritative voice notes a reason for the meeting that the president's statement omitted, and a female news anchor reports the New York Times publishing details about a campaign meeting involving a Kremlin-linked lawyer. As the authoritative voice states that 'it didn't take long for the truth to come out' upon the president's return to Washington, a wide shot shows a helicopter flying over Washington D.C., with the Washington Monument visible, before landing on the White House lawn. The final scene shows Trump, in a dark suit and red tie, stepping down from the helicopter with the Presidential Seal visible, greeted by personnel, while an energetic news anchor announces 'explosive news about President Trump and Russia,' involving Donald.
